574	
575	  /**
576	   * Resolve a writ id prefix to the full id. Mirrors ratchet's resolveId
577	   * for clicks — callers may pass the short display form (e.g. `w-mo2xi6pt`)
578	   * and receive the full id back. Throws when no writ matches or when the
579	   * prefix is ambiguous.
580	   */
581	  resolveId(prefix: string): Promise<string>;
582	
583	  /**
584	   * List writs with optional filters, ordered by createdAt descending.
585	   */
586	  list(filters?: WritFilters): Promise<WritDoc[]>;
587	
588	  /**
589	   * Count writs matching optional filters.
590	   */
591	  count(filters?: WritFilters): Promise<number>;
592	
593	  /**
594	   * Count writs currently in any `active`-classified state across every
595	   * registered writ type.
596	   *
597	   * Walks the writ-type registry on each call (no caching) and composes a
598	   * per-type OR-form query of the shape `[type, phase IN [active-states-of-type]]`.
599	   * Returns `0` when the registry is empty, when no registered type
600	   * declares any `active` state, or when no rows match.
601	   *
602	   * This is the classification-driven primitive behind the Reckoner's
603	   * drain detector and any future apparatus that needs a multi-type
604	   * "is anything in flight?" count. Existing per-phase counts (e.g.
605	   * `count({ phase: 'open' })`) remain available for type-specific reads.
606	   */
607	  countActive(): Promise<number>;
608	
609	  /**
610	   * Walk the writ hierarchy and return a forest (or a single subtree when
611	   * `rootId` is provided). Mirrors ratchet's `tree()` precedent.
612	   *
613	   * Filters apply with prune semantics: a node failing `phase` or `type`
614	   * is excluded together with its entire subtree. The `depth` cap halts
615	   * recursion below — the node at the cap is still included, but no
616	   * descendants of it are walked. `rootLimit` / `rootOffset` page across
617	   * the root layer of forest mode and are ignored when `rootId` is given.
618	   */
619	  tree(params?: WritTreeParams): Promise<WritTree[]>;
620	
621	  /**
622	   * Count all descendants of a writ grouped by phase.
623	   *
624	   * Walks the parent/child tree recursively beneath `writId`, tallying each
625	   * descendant's phase. The root writ (the writ identified by `writId`) is
626	   * excluded from the count — only its descendants contribute. The result is
627	   * a plain object keyed by `WritPhase` with numeric values; phases with no
628	   * matching descendants are simply absent.
629	   *
630	   * This is the traversal primitive behind `writ-show`'s `children.summary`
631	   * field, and is also reusable by any future subtree-oriented tooling (e.g.
632	   * a `writ-tree` apparatus). Throws if the writ does not exist.
633	   */
634	  countDescendantsByPhase(writId: string): Promise<Record<WritPhase, number>>;
635	
636	  /**
637	   * Transition a writ to a new phase, optionally setting additional fields.
638	   * Validates that the transition is legal.
639	   *
640	   * Managed fields supplied in `fields` are silently dropped before the
641	   * patch is applied: `id`, `phase`, `status`, `createdAt`, `updatedAt`,
642	   * `resolvedAt`, and `parentId`. `phase` is managed by the state machine;
643	   * the plugin-owned observation slot `status` is writable only via
644	   * `setWritStatus()` (the one sanctioned slot-write path) so that
645	   * sibling sub-slots are preserved under concurrent writers. The generic
646	   * `put()` / `patch()` paths are not supported slot-write mechanisms.
647	   */
648	  transition(id: string, to: WritPhase, fields?: Partial<WritDoc>): Promise<WritDoc>;
649	
650	  /**
651	   * Write a plugin-owned sub-slot of the writ's observation `status` map.
652	   *
653	   * The slot is the "status" side of the spec/status split: plugins publish
654	   * post-hoc observations here (stuck causes, gate state, provenance, …)
655	   * without polluting the Clerk-owned `phase` state machine.
656	   *
657	   * The call is a transactional read-modify-write — the sub-slot keyed by
658	   * `pluginId` is replaced with `value`, but sibling sub-slots owned by
659	   * other plugins are preserved. Writes emit CDC update events like any
660	   * other field change, and survive terminal phase transitions.
661	   *
662	   * Ownership is convention-only: plugin `X` writes only `status[X]`.
663	   * There is no runtime guard.
664	   *
665	   * Returns the updated writ document.
666	   */
667	  setWritStatus(writId: string, pluginId: string, value: unknown): Promise<WritDoc>;
668	
669	  /**
670	   * Write a plugin-owned sub-slot of the writ's metadata `ext` map.
671	   *
672	   * The slot is the metadata sibling to `status`: plugins attach
673	   * metadata-shape data here (petition ids, cross-references,
674	   * classifier tags, configuration extensions) — data that belongs to
675	   * the writ as an attribute of its identity, rather than the post-hoc
676	   * observation `setWritStatus` records.
677	   *
678	   * The call is a transactional read-modify-write — the sub-slot keyed by
679	   * `pluginId` is replaced with `value`, but sibling sub-slots owned by
680	   * other plugins are preserved. Writes emit CDC update events like any
681	   * other field change, and survive terminal phase transitions.
682	   *
683	   * Validation: throws when `writId` is empty, when `pluginId` is empty,
684	   * or when no writ matches `writId`. The `value` is opaque — the Clerk
685	   * does not validate sub-slot contents (mirrors `setWritStatus`).
686	   *
687	   * Each call bumps `updatedAt` to the current ISO timestamp so CDC
688	   * observers keying on the timestamp see the change.
689	   *
690	   * Ownership is convention-only: plugin `X` writes only `ext[X]`.
691	   * There is no runtime guard.
692	   *
693	   * Returns the updated writ document.
694	   */
695	  setWritExt(writId: string, pluginId: string, value: unknown): Promise<WritDoc>;
696	
697	  /**
698	   * Create a typed directional link from one writ to another.
699	   * Both writs must exist. Self-links are rejected. The `label` argument is
700	   * normalized before the composite id is constructed, so variant spellings
701	   * of the same label collapse to a single link. When a link with the same
702	   * canonical composite id already exists, the existing row is returned;
703	   * if `kind` is supplied, it is written onto the existing row (upsert). The
704	   * optional `kind` must reference an id registered in the kit-contributed

709	    targetId: string,
710	    label: string,

712	  ): Promise<WritLinkDoc>;
713	
714	  /**

717	   */
718	  links(writId: string): Promise<WritLinks>;

720	  /**
721	   * Remove a link. Idempotent — no error if the link does not exist.
722	   */
723	  unlink(sourceId: string, targetId: string, label: string): Promise<void>;
724	
725	  /**
726	   * Edit a writ. Title and body can be edited in any status.
727	   * Type and codex can only be changed while the writ is in 'new' status.
728	   * Only the provided fields are updated. Validates type against

730	   */
731	  edit(request: EditWritRequest): Promise<WritDoc>;
732	
733	  /**
734	   * List the writ types registered with the Clerk via `registerWritType`,
735	   * including the Clerk's own built-in `mandate`. Each entry reports the

739	  listWritTypes(): WritTypeInfo[];

745	   */
746	  listKinds(): Promise<LinkKindDoc[]>;
747

750	   *

753	   * specific failures (duplicate name, late call after the startup window has

755	   *

export interface ClerkApi {
  /**
   * Post a new commission, creating a writ in its registered type's
   * declared `initial` state. The writ type must be registered with
   * `registerWritType`; an unknown type is rejected. The caller decides
   * whether to advance the writ further (e.g. `commission-post` auto-
   * publishes mandate writs to `open` by default — see that tool); the
   * `post()` API itself is type-agnostic and never auto-advances.
   */
  post(request: PostCommissionRequest): Promise<WritDoc>;

  /**
   * Show a writ by id. Throws if not found.
   */
  show(id: string): Promise<WritDoc>;

  /**
   * Resolve a writ id prefix to the full id. Mirrors ratchet's resolveId
   * for clicks — callers may pass the short display form (e.g. `w-mo2xi6pt`)
   * and receive the full id back. Throws when no writ matches or when the
   * prefix is ambiguous.
   */
  resolveId(prefix: string): Promise<string>;

  /**
   * List writs with optional filters, ordered by createdAt descending.
   */
  list(filters?: WritFilters): Promise<WritDoc[]>;

  /**
   * Count writs matching optional filters.
   */
  count(filters?: WritFilters): Promise<number>;

  /**
   * Count writs currently in any `active`-classified state across every
   * registered writ type.
   *
   * Walks the writ-type registry on each call (no caching) and composes a
   * per-type OR-form query of the shape `[type, phase IN [active-states-of-type]]`.
   * Returns `0` when the registry is empty, when no registered type
   * declares any `active` state, or when no rows match.
   *
   * This is the classification-driven primitive behind the Reckoner's
   * drain detector and any future apparatus that needs a multi-type
   * "is anything in flight?" count. Existing per-phase counts (e.g.
   * `count({ phase: 'open' })`) remain available for type-specific reads.
   */
  countActive(): Promise<number>;

  /**
   * Walk the writ hierarchy and return a forest (or a single subtree when
   * `rootId` is provided). Mirrors ratchet's `tree()` precedent.
   *
   * Filters apply with prune semantics: a node failing `phase` or `type`
   * is excluded together with its entire subtree. The `depth` cap halts
   * recursion below — the node at the cap is still included, but no
   * descendants of it are walked. `rootLimit` / `rootOffset` page across
   * the root layer of forest mode and are ignored when `rootId` is given.
   */
  tree(params?: WritTreeParams): Promise<WritTree[]>;

  /**
   * Count all descendants of a writ grouped by phase.
   *
   * Walks the parent/child tree recursively beneath `writId`, tallying each
   * descendant's phase. The root writ (the writ identified by `writId`) is
   * excluded from the count — only its descendants contribute. The result is
   * a plain object keyed by `WritPhase` with numeric values; phases with no
   * matching descendants are simply absent.
   *
   * This is the traversal primitive behind `writ-show`'s `children.summary`
   * field, and is also reusable by any future subtree-oriented tooling (e.g.
   * a `writ-tree` apparatus). Throws if the writ does not exist.
   */
  countDescendantsByPhase(writId: string): Promise<Record<WritPhase, number>>;

  /**
   * Transition a writ to a new phase, optionally setting additional fields.
   * Validates that the transition is legal.
   *
   * Managed fields supplied in `fields` are silently dropped before the
   * patch is applied: `id`, `phase`, `status`, `createdAt`, `updatedAt`,
   * `resolvedAt`, and `parentId`. `phase` is managed by the state machine;
   * the plugin-owned observation slot `status` is writable only via
   * `setWritStatus()` (the one sanctioned slot-write path) so that
   * sibling sub-slots are preserved under concurrent writers. The generic
   * `put()` / `patch()` paths are not supported slot-write mechanisms.
   */
  transition(id: string, to: WritPhase, fields?: Partial<WritDoc>): Promise<WritDoc>;

  /**
   * Write a plugin-owned sub-slot of the writ's observation `status` map.
   *
   * The slot is the "status" side of the spec/status split: plugins publish
   * post-hoc observations here (stuck causes, gate state, provenance, …)
   * without polluting the Clerk-owned `phase` state machine.
   *
   * The call is a transactional read-modify-write — the sub-slot keyed by
   * `pluginId` is replaced with `value`, but sibling sub-slots owned by
   * other plugins are preserved. Writes emit CDC update events like any
   * other field change, and survive terminal phase transitions.
   *
   * Ownership is convention-only: plugin `X` writes only `status[X]`.
   * There is no runtime guard.
   *
   * Returns the updated writ document.
   */
  setWritStatus(writId: string, pluginId: string, value: unknown): Promise<WritDoc>;

  /**
   * Write a plugin-owned sub-slot of the writ's metadata `ext` map.
   *
   * The slot is the metadata sibling to `status`: plugins attach
   * metadata-shape data here (petition ids, cross-references,
   * classifier tags, configuration extensions) — data that belongs to
   * the writ as an attribute of its identity, rather than the post-hoc
   * observation `setWritStatus` records.
   *
   * The call is a transactional read-modify-write — the sub-slot keyed by
   * `pluginId` is replaced with `value`, but sibling sub-slots owned by
   * other plugins are preserved. Writes emit CDC update events like any
   * other field change, and survive terminal phase transitions.
   *
   * Validation: throws when `writId` is empty, when `pluginId` is empty,
   * or when no writ matches `writId`. The `value` is opaque — the Clerk
   * does not validate sub-slot contents (mirrors `setWritStatus`).
   *
   * Each call bumps `updatedAt` to the current ISO timestamp so CDC
   * observers keying on the timestamp see the change.
   *
   * Ownership is convention-only: plugin `X` writes only `ext[X]`.
   * There is no runtime guard.
   *
   * Returns the updated writ document.
   */
  setWritExt(writId: string, pluginId: string, value: unknown): Promise<WritDoc>;

  /**
   * Create a typed directional link from one writ to another.
   * Both writs must exist. Self-links are rejected. The `label` argument is
   * normalized before the composite id is constructed, so variant spellings
   * of the same label collapse to a single link. When a link with the same
   * canonical composite id already exists, the existing row is returned;
   * if `kind` is supplied, it is written onto the existing row (upsert). The
   * optional `kind` must reference an id registered in the kit-contributed
   * link-kind registry; unknown ids are rejected.
   */
  link(
    sourceId: string,
    targetId: string,
    label: string,
    kind?: string,
  ): Promise<WritLinkDoc>;

  /**
   * Query all links for a writ — both outbound (this writ is the source)
   * and inbound (this writ is the target).
   */
  links(writId: string): Promise<WritLinks>;

  /**
   * Remove a link. Idempotent — no error if the link does not exist.
   */
  unlink(sourceId: string, targetId: string, label: string): Promise<void>;

  /**
   * Edit a writ. Title and body can be edited in any status.
   * Type and codex can only be changed while the writ is in 'new' status.
   * Only the provided fields are updated. Validates type against
   * declared writ types if provided.
   */
  edit(request: EditWritRequest): Promise<WritDoc>;

  /**
   * List the writ types registered with the Clerk via `registerWritType`,
   * including the Clerk's own built-in `mandate`. Each entry reports the
   * name, the (currently-always-null) description, source (`builtin` or
   * `plugin`), and whether it is the default type.
   */
  listWritTypes(): WritTypeInfo[];

  /**
   * List all registered link kinds contributed by kits.
   * Returns the registry-projection view of each kind, including its owner
   * plugin id. Returns an empty array when no kinds have been registered.
   */
  listKinds(): Promise<LinkKindDoc[]>;

  /**
   * Register a writ type's state machine with the Clerk.
   *
   * The supplied config is validated via `validateWritTypeConfig` before the
   * registry is updated; validator errors propagate verbatim. Registration-
   * specific failures (duplicate name, late call after the startup window has
   * sealed) are wrapped with a `[clerk] registerWritType:` prefix.
   *
   * Callable only while the startup window is open — Clerk seals the
   * registry on the framework's global `phase:started` signal, after which
   * further calls throw. This is the single-surface entry point for plugins

11	import type {
12	  StartupContext,
13	  Plugin,
14	} from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
15	
16	// ── Public types ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
17	
18	/** Minimal execution context passed to an engine's run() method. */
19	export interface EngineRunContext {
20	  /** The rig this engine instance belongs to. */
21	  rigId: string;
22	  /** Simple string identity for this engine instance (e.g. 'draft', 'implement'). */
23	  engineId: string;
24	  /** All upstream yields, keyed by engine id. Escape hatch for engines that need to inspect the full upstream chain. */
25	  upstream: Record<string, unknown>;
26	  /**
27	   * Present when this engine was previously blocked and has been restarted.
28	   * Advisory — do not depend on for correctness.
29	   *
30	   * Note: Defined inline to avoid a circular package dependency with spider-apparatus.
31	   * Shape matches spider-apparatus BlockRecord exactly.
32	   */
33	  priorBlock?: {
34	    type: string;
35	    condition: unknown;
36	    blockedAt: string;
37	    message?: string;
38	    lastCheckedAt?: string;
39	  };
40	}
41	
42	/**
43	 * The result of an engine run.
44	 *
45	 * 'completed' — synchronous work done inline, yields are available immediately.
46	 * 'launched'  — async work launched in a session; the Spider polls for completion.
47	 * 'blocked'   — engine is waiting for an external condition; Spider will poll
48	 *               the registered block type's checker and restart when cleared.
49	 */
50	export type EngineRunResult =
51	  | { status: 'completed'; yields: unknown }
52	  | { status: 'launched'; sessionId: string }
53	  | { status: 'blocked'; blockType: string; condition: unknown; message?: string };
54	
55	/**
56	 * Back-off growth parameters for an engine's retry policy. Matches
57	 * the shape Animator uses for its rate-limit back-off:
58	 *   - initialMs — first hold window in milliseconds
59	 *   - maxMs    — cap on the hold window in milliseconds
60	 *   - factor   — growth multiplier per consumed attempt
61	 */
62	export interface EngineRetryBackoffConfig {
63	  initialMs: number;
64	  maxMs: number;
65	  factor: number;
66	}
67	
68	/**
69	 * Opt-in retry policy for an engine design. When absent, the effective
70	 * policy is `maxAttempts: 0` — the engine fails terminally on the first
71	 * transient error with no retry. Explicit config enables retry.
72	 *
73	 * The Spider validates this shape at engine-design registration time
74	 * (see `validateEngineRetryConfig`); malformed values throw at startup.
75	 */
76	export interface EngineRetryConfig {
77	  /**
78	   * Total retry budget. `0` means fail-fast (no retry). `1` means one
79	   * retry (so up to two attempts total). Matches the "attempts consumed
80	   * from budget" semantics.
81	   */
82	  maxAttempts: number;
83	  /** Back-off growth parameters. Optional; defaults applied when omitted. */
84	  backoff?: Partial<EngineRetryBackoffConfig>;
85	}
86	
87	/**
88	 * An engine design — the unit of work the Fabricator catalogues and the
89	 * Spider executes. Kit authors import this type from @shardworks/fabricator-apparatus.
90	 */
91	export interface EngineDesign {
92	  /** Unique identifier for this engine design (e.g. 'draft', 'implement', 'review'). */
93	  id: string;
94	
95	  /**
96	   * Execute this engine.
97	   *
98	   * @param givens   — the engine's declared inputs, assembled by the Spider.
99	   * @param context  — minimal execution context: engine id and upstream yields.
100	   */
101	  run(givens: Record<string, unknown>, context: EngineRunContext): Promise<EngineRunResult>;
102	
103	  /**
104	   * Assemble yields from a completed session.
105	   *
106	   * Called by the Spider's collect step when a quick engine's session
107	   * reaches a terminal state. The engine looks up whatever it needs
108	   * via guild() — same dependency pattern as run().
109	   *
110	   * @param sessionId — the session to collect yields from (primary input).
111	   * @param givens    — same givens that were passed to run().
112	   * @param context   — same execution context that was passed to run().
113	   *
114	   * If not defined, the Spider uses a generic default:
115	   *   { sessionId, sessionStatus, output? }
116	   *
117	   * Only relevant for quick engines (those that return { status: 'launched' }).
118	   * Clockwork engines return yields directly from run().
119	   */
120	  collect?(sessionId: string, givens: Record<string, unknown>, context: EngineRunContext): Promise<unknown>;
121	
122	  /**
123	   * Opt-in retry policy. When absent, the engine has no retry budget
124	   * (effectively `maxAttempts: 0`). Validated at registration — a
125	   * malformed block throws at startup.
126	   */
127	  retry?: EngineRetryConfig;
128	}
129	
130	// ── Retry config defaults and validation ──────────────────────────────
131	
132	/**
133	 * Default back-off values applied when a retry-enabled engine design
134	 * omits explicit back-off. Chosen short — retry is a rig-local mechanism,
135	 * not a long-term scheduler. 30s initial, 10m cap, 2x growth.
136	 */
137	export const DEFAULT_ENGINE_RETRY_BACKOFF: EngineRetryBackoffConfig = Object.freeze({
138	  initialMs: 30_000,
139	  maxMs: 600_000,

# c-moma9llq-195259699140 [live]
> Implementation — turn the Laboratory MVP0 design into a working apparatus. Children decompose the build into commissionable units; each child becomes a brief + posted commission when ready to dispatch. First few items (framework verification, plugin reshape, schema/CLI) unblock everything else; engines and archive can parallelize once the skeleton lands.

Confirm gh org permissions for codex repo create/delete. Surface gaps to negotiate with framework before scripts land.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: All load-bearing prerequisites confirmed against live framework source: non-anima engines first-class via { status: 'completed' } EngineRunResult (precedent: spider.seal does git ops sync and returns yields); 'nsg plugin install' accepts SHAs natively via npm git+URL#sha syntax; ClerkApi.registerWritType is the writ-type contribution path (called from apparatus.start); SpiderKit.rigTemplates + rigTemplateMappings contribute templates+routings; Fabricator scans kit.engines for designs. One spec correction: 'nsg codex add' takes only (name, remoteUrl) — no --sha flag; pinning happens earlier by pushing fresh GH repo at the desired SHA before codex-add. One yellow flag: gh token has 'repo' scope and active org membership in shardworks but admin=false on the repo; member-policy for repo create/delete unverified — must test on first smoke run. Patron decisions: codex repos go in shardworks org, --private, naming 'trial-<slug>-<trialId-prefix>'. Findings doc: .scratch/laboratory-framework-recon.md (transient; folds into architecture doc per c-momaaa3t).

> Author manifest YAML format and the 'nsg lab trial post --manifest <file>' CLI — manifest schema (same shape as ext.laboratory.config plus optional 'description' field), parser, validator, writ-post via existing CLI surfaces.

Conclusion: Manifest CLI landed: src/manifest.ts (zod schema mirroring LaboratoryTrialConfig with optional title, description, parentId, codex), parser (YAML → schema validation → fixture DAG cross-validation via topoSortFixtures → probe id uniqueness), and manifestToWritShape (splits into clerk.post inputs + ext.laboratory.config payload). src/tools/trial-post.ts wraps the parser and chains: clerk.post(type='trial') → clerk.setWritExt('laboratory', {config}) → clerk.transition('new'→'open') unless --draft. Codex auto-resolution mirrors commission-post (inherit from parentId; default to single registered codex; fail when multiple/none). 26 manifest tests added (55 total): YAML parse errors, schema rejections (slug shape, missing required, malformed ids, mutability enum), defaults (empty fixtures/probes, empty givens), cross-field DAG validation passthrough, probe id uniqueness, manifestToWritShape (title default 'Trial: <slug>', description→body, parentId/codex pass-through, trialConfig packing). CLI form is nsg lab-trial-post (not nsg lab trial-post); auto-grouping activates once a second lab-prefixed tool is added (framework requires ≥2 sibling tools sharing a prefix). Slug rules: lowercase kebab-case, alphanumeric+hyphen, must start with letter, ≤40 chars (keeps trial-<slug>-<trialId-prefix> codex/repo names under the 63-char DNS/GH limit with margin). SupportKit wiring complete: rigTemplates['post-and-collect-default'] + rigTemplateMappings.trial. Skeleton engines for every non-orchestrate engine design (codex/guild setup+teardown, commission-post-xguild, wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild, probe-stacks-dump, probe-git-range, archive) yield placeholder data so a posted trial crawls cleanly through the rig before any engine does real work — verifies the wiring before the implementation children land. 21 unit tests on buildGraft cover topo-sort, upstream wiring, cycle/unknown-dep rejection, tail identification, teardown-id derivation (convention + override), empty-fixtures + empty-probes variants, and given pass-through. Build/typecheck/test clean against linked live framework.

Status: concluded
Conclusion: Real implementations of lab.guild-setup + lab.guild-teardown landed (engines/guild-fixture.ts). Setup: nsg init <path> → per-pin nsg plugin install → codex registration via duck-type discovery (any upstream yield with codexName+remoteUrl gets registered) → deep-merge guild.json → file copies (absolute sourcePath only in v1; manifest-relative resolution is future polish). Teardown: archive-presence safety check + rm -rf. Both engines auto-default guildName from _trial.{slug,writId} matching the codex-fixture pattern. Default guildPath is <labHost>/.nexus/laboratory/guilds/<name>/ — lab-host-internal, mirroring codex placement (departs from the handoff's /workspace/experiments/... default; can be overridden via givens.guildPath if Sean prefers sanctum-side visibility). 27 unit tests covering pure helpers (deepMerge, discoverCodexes, defaultGuildPath) + engine validation/guards/teardown branches. ## c-momaa1vt-d6c8e41390a3 [concluded]
> Implement cross-guild scenario engines — 'commission-post-xguild' (shells out to 'nsg commission-post --guild-root <X>') and 'wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild' (polls writ status across guilds via --guild-root). Sequential commission posting only in v1; concurrent posting deferred.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: Cross-guild scenario engines landed (engines/scenario-xguild.ts). lab.commission-post-xguild posts a commission via 'nsg --guild-root <test-guild> commission-post', then INLINE polls until terminal (default waitForTerminal=true) — single engine fits the rig template's single-scenario-engine slot for the canonical 'post and wait' flow. Optional opt-out via waitForTerminal=false yields just {writId, postedAt}. Test guild discovery via duck-typing on context.upstream (any yield with {guildName, guildPath}). Title defaults to brief's first H1, then 'Commission from <basename>'. Codex auto-resolves in the test guild (single-codex case) or inherits from parentId. lab.wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild is the standalone polling engine — useful for future multi-step scenarios where post is detached from wait. Both engines share waitForWritTerminal helper which polls 'nsg writ-show --format json' and checks classification==='terminal' to detect terminal state. Defaults: pollIntervalMs=5000, timeoutMs=1.8M (30 min), fail-loud on timeout. 22 unit tests covering pure helpers (discoverTestGuilds 5 cases, extractH1Title 6 cases) + engine validation guards (briefPath absolute, no/multi guild, malformed numbers, missing brief, writId required). Subprocess happy path deferred to live smoke verification — same rationale as guild-fixture (booting nsg per test is too expensive). Live-verify pending fresh daemon restart since all three engine pairs (codex, guild, scenario) post-date the daemon's loaded code.

Each probe owns its book schema, run() handler, and extract() handler under src/probes/<name>/, and registers with the probe registry (c-momkil4p). Spec: packages/laboratory/README.md.

## c-momaa5o9-62fbf19d185d [concluded]
> Design and implement the archive engine — open sub-question on output target (lab guild books vs sanctum mirror vs hybrid?), per-trial storage layout, linkage from trial writ to archive artifacts. Design conclusion lands in this click; implementation follows in same scope.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: DB-authoritative archive with on-demand filesystem materialization. Archive engine owns one minimal index book — lab-trial-archives — with row shape { id, trialId (FK to clerk/writs), status, archivedAt, probes[{id, engineId, summary}] }; archive engine has no schema opinions about probe data. Probes own their own books: probe-stacks-dump writes generic-bodied rows (one per source-row across test-guild books) to lab-trial-stacks-dumps; probe-git-range writes one row per captured commit (10MB per-diff hard-cap tripwire) to lab-trial-codex-commits. A new probe — lab.probe-trial-context — joins the standard probe set and captures rig id, rig template name, framework SHA, resolved plugin pins, and manifest snapshot as summary-only data (no bulk book). Trial-writ linkage is FK-only (lab-trial-archives.trialId) — no clerk link kind, since clerk links are writ-to-writ. Pure JSON1 expression indexes added per hot query (no per-row extracted columns). Atomicity via single SQLite transaction. Annotations and analysis live in sanctum markdown referencing trialId — not in any laboratory book. Probes and their books bundle in @shardworks/laboratory for MVP with code seams (per-probe directories) designed for clean v2 extraction into separate plugins. Materialization via 'nsg lab trial-extract <trialId> --to <dir>' uses a probe registry that dispatches by engineId — MVP-required, tracked at sibling click c-momkil4p. CLI surface: nsg lab trial-show, nsg lab trial-extract, nsg lab trial-export-book. Full spec landed in packages/laboratory/README.md.

CLI form is nsg lab-trial-post (not nsg lab trial-post); auto-grouping activates once a second lab-prefixed tool is added (framework requires ≥2 sibling tools sharing a prefix). ## c-momaa5o9-62fbf19d185d [concluded]
> Design and implement the archive engine — open sub-question on output target (lab guild books vs sanctum mirror vs hybrid?), per-trial storage layout, linkage from trial writ to archive artifacts. Design conclusion lands in this click; implementation follows in same scope.
Status: concluded
Conclusion: DB-authoritative archive with on-demand filesystem materialization. Archive engine owns one minimal index book — lab-trial-archives — with row shape { id, trialId (FK to clerk/writs), status, archivedAt, probes[{id, engineId, summary}] }; archive engine has no schema opinions about probe data. Probes own their own books: probe-stacks-dump writes generic-bodied rows (one per source-row across test-guild books) to lab-trial-stacks-dumps; probe-git-range writes one row per captured commit (10MB per-diff hard-cap tripwire) to lab-trial-codex-commits. A new probe — lab.probe-trial-context — joins the standard probe set and captures rig id, rig template name, framework SHA, resolved plugin pins, and manifest snapshot as summary-only data (no bulk book). Trial-writ linkage is FK-only (lab-trial-archives.trialId) — no clerk link kind, since clerk links are writ-to-writ. Pure JSON1 expression indexes added per hot query (no per-row extracted columns). Atomicity via single SQLite transaction. Annotations and analysis live in sanctum markdown referencing trialId — not in any laboratory book. Probes and their books bundle in @shardworks/laboratory for MVP with code seams (per-probe directories) designed for clean v2 extraction into separate plugins. Materialization via 'nsg lab trial-extract <trialId> --to <dir>' uses a probe registry that dispatches by engineId — MVP-required, tracked at sibling click c-momkil4p. CLI surface: nsg lab trial-show, nsg lab trial-extract, nsg lab trial-export-book. Full spec landed in packages/laboratory/README.md.

180	 * ordered pipeline of engine instances. The Spider updates this document
181	 * in-place as engines run and complete.
182	 */
183	export interface RigDoc {
184	  /** Index signature required to satisfy BookEntry constraint. */
185	  [key: string]: unknown;
186	  /** Unique rig id. */
187	  id: string;
188	  /** The writ this rig is executing. */
189	  writId: string;
190	  /** Current rig status. */
191	  status: RigStatus;
192	  /** Ordered engine pipeline. */
193	  engines: EngineInstance[];
194	  /** ISO timestamp when the rig was created. */
195	  createdAt: string;
196	  /**
197	   * ISO timestamp recorded the first time the rig enters a terminal status
198	   * (`completed`, `failed`, `cancelled`). Keep-first semantics:
199	   * subsequent terminal transitions do NOT overwrite this value — it
200	   * pins the moment the rig first stopped making forward progress.
201	   * Absent on rigs that predate this field; the dashboard falls back to
202	   * the latest `attempts[-1].endedAt` for those.
203	   */
204	  terminalAt?: string;
205	  /**
206	   * ISO timestamp recorded when the rig is cancelled by explicit operator
207	   * action (via `SpiderApi.cancel`). The rig-status rollup short-circuits
208	   * to `'cancelled'` when this is set, distinguishing operator-cancel
209	   * from cascade-by-upstream cancel.
210	   */
211	  cancelledAt?: string;
212	  /** Engine id whose yields provide the resolution summary. Set at spawn time. */
213	  resolutionEngineId?: string;
214	}
215	
216	// ── Rig view (UI-facing read shape) ───────────────────────────────────
217	
218	/**
219	 * Aggregated cost summary for a rig — sum across all engines that have a
220	 * sessionId. Absent when no engines have reported any cost data yet.
221	 *
222	 * Token counts are optional because not every provider/session reports
223	 * token usage. When tokenUsage is absent on every contributing session,
224	 * `inputTokens` / `outputTokens` will be undefined; the UI uses this to
225	 * decide whether to render the `(N input, M output)` parenthetical.
226	 */
227	export interface RigCostSummary {
228	  /** Total cost in USD across all sessions. */
229	  costUsd: number;
230	  /** Sum of input tokens across all sessions that reported tokenUsage. Undefined if no session reported. */
231	  inputTokens?: number;
232	  /** Sum of output tokens across all sessions that reported tokenUsage. Undefined if no session reported. */
233	  outputTokens?: number;
234	}
235	
236	/**
237	 * Per-engine cost snapshot. Same shape as the rig-level `RigCostSummary`.
238	 * Present in the `engineCosts` map for every engine that has a sessionId
239	 * (regardless of engine status) — the value may be all-zero when the
240	 * session has not reported cost yet.
241	 */
242	export interface EngineCostSummary {
243	  /** Cost in USD from the engine's session, or 0 if not reported. */
244	  costUsd: number;
245	  /** Input tokens if reported. */
246	  inputTokens?: number;
247	  /** Output tokens if reported. */
248	  outputTokens?: number;
249	}
250	
251	/**
252	 * UI-facing rig view — the persisted `RigDoc` enriched with derived fields
253	 * that the Spider dashboard needs. Returned from the rig API read path
254	 * (rig-list / rig-show). The persisted `RigDoc` shape is unchanged.
255	 *
256	 * - `costSummary` is the rig-level aggregate (sum across all engines with
257	 *   a sessionId). Omitted when no engine has a sessionId.
258	 * - `engineCosts` maps engineId → per-engine cost snapshot. Contains an
259	 *   entry for every engine with a sessionId. Omitted when no engine has
260	 *   a sessionId.
261	 * - `writTitle` is the current title of this rig's writ, joined from the
262	 *   `clerk/writs` book. Omitted when the writ cannot be resolved (e.g.
263	 *   deleted). Derived-only — never persisted on `RigDoc`; recomputed on
264	 *   every read so `writ-edit` title changes are reflected on the next
265	 *   dashboard poll.
266	 *
267	 * All derived fields are read-only reports — they are never persisted.
268	 */
269	export interface RigView extends RigDoc {
270	  /** Rig-level cost aggregate (sum across all engine sessions). */
271	  costSummary?: RigCostSummary;
272	  /** Per-engine cost snapshot keyed by engine id. */
273	  engineCosts?: Record<string, EngineCostSummary>;
274	  /** Current title of this rig's writ, joined from `clerk/writs`. */
275	  writTitle?: string;
276	}

495	// ── SpiderApi ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
496	
497	/**
498	 * The Spider's public API — retrieved via guild().apparatus<SpiderApi>('spider').
499	 */
500	export interface SpiderApi {
501	  /**
502	   * Execute one step of the crawl loop.
503	   *
504	   * Priority ordering: collect > graft > run > spawn.
505	   * Returns null when no work is available.
506	   */
507	  crawl(): Promise<CrawlResult | null>;
508	
509	  /**
510	   * Show a rig by id. Throws if not found.
511	   */
512	  show(id: string): Promise<RigDoc>;
513	
514	  /**
515	   * List rigs with optional filters, ordered by createdAt descending.
516	   */
517	  list(filters?: RigFilters): Promise<RigDoc[]>;
518	
519	  /**
520	   * Find the rig for a given writ. Returns null if no rig exists.
521	   */
522	  forWrit(writId: string): Promise<RigDoc | null>;
523	
524	  /**
525	   * Clear a hold on a specific pending engine, forcing the dispatch
526	   * predicate to re-evaluate it on the next crawl tick. Throws if the
527	   * engine is not pending or has no hold set.
528	   */
529	  resume(rigId: string, engineId: string): Promise<void>;
530	
531	  /**
532	   * Cancel a running rig. Cancels the active session (if any), marks
533	   * all non-terminal engines as cancelled, rejects pending input
534	   * requests, and transitions the rig to cancelled status.
535	   *
536	   * Idempotent: returns the rig unchanged if it is already in a terminal state.
537	   * Throws if the rig is not found.
538	   */
539	  cancel(rigId: string, options?: { reason?: string }): Promise<RigDoc>;
540	
541	  /**
542	   * Look up a registered block type by ID.
543	   */
544	  getBlockType(id: string): BlockType | undefined;
545	
546	  /**
547	   * List all registered block types with summary info.
548	   */
549	  listBlockTypes(): BlockTypeInfo[];
550	
551	  /**
552	   * List all registered rig templates with provenance info.
553	   */
554	  listTemplates(): RigTemplateInfo[];

- session: cb9f1554-a5f1-4144-88e9-6170b3cc653b
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "Live verification of the three implemented engine pairs against vibers (after Sean restarted the daemon). Two trials posted and completed. Trial 1 (w-momj4qcu, rig rig-momj4tf8-1702153d, 14s wallclock): codex auto-default → guild-setup with plugin install (tools+codexes via file:<path> npm syntax — link: is pnpm-only and trips EUNSUPPORTEDPROTOCOL on npm guilds) → codex registration in test guild → stub scenario+archive → teardown chain. Codex name auto-derived as `verify-fixtures-a5d9ea57` (slug + writId tail-8). Trial 2 (w-momj78c5, rig rig-momj7a34-139e5044, 13.5s wallclock): same setup PLUS clerk+stacks plugins, then real lab.commission-post-xguild posting a brief to the test guild with waitForTerminal=false, then teardown. Both trials ran 11 engines each in single attempts. Verified end-to-end: _trial injection (slug+writId+fixtureId via phases.ts at graft time), codex auto-default, guild auto-default, codex registration via duck-typed upstream discovery (any {codexName, remoteUrl} yield gets `nsg codex add` in the test guild), test-guild discovery via {guildName, guildPath} duck-typing in commission-post-xguild, plugin install loop (sequential, file:<path> for npm-mode link installs), commission-post shell-out + JSON parse, teardown chain order (guild-teardown before codex-teardown via reverse topo). Not live-verified but unit-test-covered: waitForTerminal=true polling path, standalone wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild engine, deep-merge guild.json config, file copies. Two pieces of grit surfaced: (1) Failed trials leave orphan codexes — when guild-setup fails after codex-setup succeeds, the rig fails without running teardowns, leaving the codex registered in the lab-host's guild.json. Worth a follow-up click — teardown should still run on rig failure to clean up upstream side effects, OR setup engines should grab a release-on-rig-terminal handle. (2) Daemon's Scriptorium has stale in-memory state vs guild.json after CLI-side `nsg codex remove` invocations — the CLI runs in a separate process with its own Scriptorium that updates guild.json, but the daemon's in-memory state isn't notified, and the daemon's next guild.json write reverts the orphans. Cosmetic for now (orphan codexes don't run any code), but worth flagging."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 0cb4907e-adf3-41d2-92f3-6e88dc76565b
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "Resolved the laboratory archive design (concludes c-momaa5o9). Decision: DB-authoritative archive with on-demand filesystem materialization — captured trial data lives in the lab guild's stacks DB, the filesystem story is provided by an extract tool that reads from the DB on demand. Pivoted from my initial hybrid (C) recommendation after Sean reframed the brittleness concern as 'patron-tree↔apparatus-data' rather than 'DB↔filesystem' abstractly — the sanctum is human-owned text under git, lab dumps are machine-owned bulk data, mixing them was the actual smell. Three books: lab-trial-archives (archive-engine-owned index, one row per trial, shape {id, trialId-FK, status, archivedAt, probes[{id, engineId, summary}]}, archive engine has no schema opinions about probe data) + lab-trial-stacks-dumps (probe-stacks-dump-owned, generic JSON-bodied row per source-row across test-guild books, queried via JSON1 expression indexes added per hot pattern) + lab-trial-codex-commits (probe-git-range-owned, one row per captured commit with diff body, 10MB per-diff hard-cap tripwire). New probe joining the standard set: lab.probe-trial-context — captures rig id, rig template name, framework SHA, resolved plugin pins, manifest snapshot as summary-only data; folds into c-momaa3w7 implementation scope. Trial-writ linkage is FK-only (caught a real flaw — clerk's WritLinkDoc is writ-to-writ only via sourceId/targetId; archive records aren't writs, so my earlier 'laboratory.archived-as' link kind couldn't have worked). CLI surface: nsg lab trial-show / trial-extract / trial-export-book (renamed from export-jsonl). Probe-extraction dispatch via a probe registry — Sean rejected MVP-hardcoding ('the type of thing we will forget the intent by the time we need to add the lookup'), so the registry is MVP-required, opened as new sibling click c-momkil4p under c-moma9llq. Bundled-with-clean-seams packaging for MVP: probes and their books all live in @shardworks/laboratory but organized per-probe (src/probes/<name>/{engine.ts, book.ts, extractor.ts}) so future per-plugin extraction is a mechanical lift. Atomicity via single SQLite transaction; teardown gate on archive status==='complete'. Annotations live sanctum-side referencing trialId, not in any laboratory book. Full spec written to packages/laboratory/README.md as the canonical home. Conversational arc: pressure-tested A/B/C with worked example of a P3 trial, recommended C, took Sean's reframe to A, then took three more Sean pokes (scenarioWritId being scenario-specific, codex URL belongs on writ not duplicated, metadata-as-flat-bag mixing probe-yields with apparatus-facts, books being too specific) that revealed the deeper architectural fix — archive engine is a minimal index, probes own their own books and extraction. Companion handoff (.scratch/handoff-laboratory-implementation.md) retained as the next thread's anchor; archive-design handoff scratch deleted now that the spec is in its permanent home."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 0cb4907e-adf3-41d2-92f3-6e88dc76565b
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "Tightened archive design before implementation handoff — caught a real architectural inconsistency in my README v1: the 'single SQLite transaction wrapping archive engine + all probes' atomicity story doesn't work given the rig template grafts probes BEFORE archive as separate engines (each engine is its own transaction by construction). Fixed: per-engine atomicity, archive row writes once on success, orphan probe rows from rig-failed trials are tolerated (every analytical query joins from lab-trial-archives so orphans don't surface). Dropped the 'status: in-progress | complete | failed' field from the LabTrialArchive schema — vestigial once we accept atomic-per-row writes; row existence IS the success signal. Teardown gate language updated: 'archive row exists for this trialId' (was 'status===complete'). Click hygiene: amended c-momaa3w7 to cover the three probes with the new storage shape (was stale 'JSON-per-table' wording from the pre-design conversation handoff); opened c-momkqtn5 as the implementation click for the archive engine itself (the original c-momaa5o9 conflated design+implementation; concluding it on design alone left implementation untracked)."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 927ab84f-c92d-4a65-94ac-a19daf97ee42
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "Implemented the three standard probes (lab.probe-trial-context, lab.probe-stacks-dump, lab.probe-git-range), the lab.archive engine, and the supporting book schemas (lab-trial-archives, lab-trial-stacks-dumps, lab-trial-codex-commits). Probe-extraction dispatch uses a type-guard pattern over the existing Fabricator engine registry (no separate probe registry needed — Sean's call). Each probe's `extract()` method is structurally added to a ProbeEngineDesign subtype of EngineDesign; trial-extract resolves probes by `getEngineDesign(id)` + `isProbeEngineDesign` type guard. Archive engine writes one atomic row to lab-trial-archives with {id, trialId, archivedAt, probes:[{id, engineId, summary}]}; trials whose rigs fail before reaching archive simply have no row. Teardown safety check tightened from 'context.upstream.archive defined' to 'archive row exists for trialId' via a shared archive/presence.ts helper that resolves trialId from _trial.writId and queries lab-trial-archives count. Stacks-dump probe opens the test guild's nexus.db read-only via better-sqlite3 (already a transitive dep), enumerates books_* tables from sqlite_master, and writes one row per source-row to lab-trial-stacks-dumps; source-book name resolution uses a normalized-prefix match against the test guild's installed plugin list. Git-range probe walks `git rev-list --reverse base..head` in the codex bare repo, captures one row per commit with diff text, and fails loud on any single diff over 10MB. Trial-context probe captures rig id, rig template, framework version (from guildConfig.nexus), pluginsInstalled, and the writ's ext.laboratory.config snapshot — summary-only, no book. New StacksTestStub helper (packages/laboratory/src/archive/test-stacks-stub.ts) gives engine tests a tiny in-memory StacksApi for archive-presence checks. 124/124 tests pass."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum
EOF

laboratory: implement three probes + archive engine

The probe-extraction dispatch contract (c-momkil4p), the three
standard probes (c-momaa3w7), and the archive engine (c-momkqtn5)
land in one cohesive change since they share the book schemas and
the supportKit wiring needs to flip atomically.

Probes:
  lab.probe-trial-context — summary-only; captures rig id, rig
    template, framework version, plugin pins, manifest snapshot.
  lab.probe-stacks-dump   — opens the test guild's nexus.db
    read-only via better-sqlite3, enumerates books_* tables, writes
    one row per source-row to lab-trial-stacks-dumps.
  lab.probe-git-range     — walks `git rev-list --reverse
    base..head` in the codex bare, writes one row per commit to
    lab-trial-codex-commits, fails loud on any diff > 10MB.

Probe registry replaced with type-guard pattern over the existing
Fabricator registry: probes implement ProbeEngineDesign (extends
EngineDesign with extract()); the trial-extract tool will resolve
via getEngineDesign(id) + isProbeEngineDesign. No separate registry.

Archive engine writes one atomic row to lab-trial-archives with
{id, trialId, archivedAt, probes:[{id, engineId, summary}]}.

Teardown safety check tightened (per c-momkqtn5):
  before:  context.upstream.archive !== undefined
  after:   archive row exists for trialId in lab-trial-archives
Shared via archive/presence.ts; trialId resolved from
framework-injected _trial.writId.

Books wired through supportKit.books at owner id 'laboratory'.

124/124 unit tests passing.

Session: 927ab84f-c92d-4a65-94ac-a19daf97ee42
EOF

content.content:
1	/**
2	 * Tool SDK — the primary authoring interface for module-based tools.
3	 *
4	 * Use `tool()` to define a typed tool with Zod parameter schemas.
5	 * The returned definition is what the MCP engine imports and registers as a tool,
6	 * what the CLI uses to auto-generate subcommands, and what engines import directly.
7	 *
8	 * A package can export a single tool or an array of tools:
9	 *
10	 * @example Single tool
11	 * ```typescript
12	 * import { tool } from '@shardworks/tools-apparatus';
13	 * import { z } from 'zod';
14	 *
15	 * export default tool({
16	 *   name: 'lookup',
17	 *   description: 'Look up an anima by name',
18	 *   instructionsFile: './instructions.md',
19	 *   params: {
20	 *     name: z.string().describe('Anima name'),
21	 *   },
22	 *   handler: async ({ name }) => {
23	 *     const { home } = guild();
24	 *     return { found: true, status: 'active' };
25	 *   },
26	 * });
27	 * ```
28	 *
29	 * @example Tool collection
30	 * ```typescript
31	 * export default [
32	 *   tool({ name: 'commission', description: '...', params: {...}, handler: ... }),
33	 *   tool({ name: 'signal', description: '...', params: {...}, handler: ... }),
34	 * ];
35	 * ```
36	 */
37	import { z } from 'zod';
38	
39	// Zod shape type — a record of string keys to Zod schemas.
40	// Using a local alias keeps our public API stable across Zod versions.
41	type ZodShape = Record<string, z.ZodType>;
42	
43	/**
44	 * The caller types a tool can be invoked by.
45	 * - `'patron'` — accessible via `nsg` commands (human-facing)
46	 * - `'anima'` — accessible via MCP server (anima-facing, in sessions)
47	 * - `'library'` — accessible programmatically via direct import
48	 *
49	 * Defaults to all caller types if `callableBy` is unspecified.
50	 */
51	export type ToolCaller = 'patron' | 'anima' | 'library';
52	
53	/**
54	 * A fully-defined tool — the return type of `tool()`.
55	 *
56	 * The MCP engine uses `.params.shape` to register the tool's input schema,
57	 * `.description` for the tool description, and `.handler` to execute calls.
58	 * The CLI uses `.params` to auto-generate Commander options.
59	 * Engines call `.handler` directly.
60	 */
61	export interface ToolDefinition<TShape extends ZodShape = ZodShape> {
62	  /** Tool name — used for resolution when a package exports multiple tools. */
63	  readonly name: string;
64	  readonly description: string;
65	  /** Per-tool instructions injected into the anima's session context (inline text). */
66	  readonly instructions?: string;
67	  /**
68	   * Path to an instructions file, relative to the package root.
69	   * Resolved by the manifest engine at session time.
70	   * Mutually exclusive with `instructions`.
71	   */
72	  readonly instructionsFile?: string;
73	  /**
74	   * Caller types this tool is available to.
75	   * Always a normalized array. Absent means available to all callers.
76	   */
77	  readonly callableBy?: ToolCaller[];
78	  /**
79	   * Permission level required to invoke this tool. Matched against role grants.
80	   *
81	   * Format: a freeform string chosen by the tool author. Conventional names:
82	   * - `'read'` — query/inspect operations
83	   * - `'write'` — create/update operations
84	   * - `'delete'` — destructive operations
85	   * - `'admin'` — configuration and lifecycle operations
86	   *
87	   * Plugins are free to define their own levels.
88	   * If omitted, the tool is permissionless — included by default in non-strict
89	   * mode, excluded in strict mode unless the role grants `plugin:*` or `*:*`.
90	   */
91	  readonly permission?: string;
92	  readonly params: z.ZodObject<TShape>;
93	  readonly handler: (
94	    params: z.infer<z.ZodObject<TShape>>,
95	  ) => unknown | Promise<unknown>;
96	}
97	
98	/** Input to `tool()` — instructions are either inline text or a file path, not both. */
99	type ToolInput<TShape extends ZodShape> = {
100	  name: string;

GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Coco GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=coco@nexus.local git -C /workspace/nexus-mk2 commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
laboratory: trial retrieval CLI tools + probe/archive tests

Three new tools land alongside the existing trial-post:

  lab-trial-show         — print archive metadata + probe summaries
                           for <trialId>.
  lab-trial-extract      — materialize captured data to a directory.
                           Dispatches per-probe extract() via Fabricator
                           lookup + isProbeEngineDesign type guard;
                           writes manifest.yaml and README.md at the
                           top level. Refuses non-empty targets without
                           --force.
  lab-trial-export-book  — stream one source book as JSONL (default)
                           or JSON for analysis pipelines.

With 4 lab-* tools registered, the framework's auto-grouping
activates and all four surface as `nsg lab <subcommand>`.

Unit tests added (36 new): probes/types.test.ts (type guard),
probes/stacks-dump.test.ts (normalizeOwnerId, resolveSourceBook,
safeFileStem), probes/git-range.test.ts (discoverCodexFixtures,
parseShortStat, patchFileName), archive/engine.test.ts
(buildProbeEntries + archive engine run()).

160/160 tests passing.

Session: 927ab84f-c92d-4a65-94ac-a19daf97ee42
EOF
)"

slug: verify-fixtures
title: "Live verify: codex + guild fixture chain (auto-defaults, _trial, codex registration in test guild)"
description: |
  Smoke test of the real codex + guild fixture engines together.
  No explicit codexName/guildName — exercises the auto-default
  paths from injected _trial context. No plugins, no config, no
  files — keeps the test fast. Scenario uses the lab.archive stub
  as a no-op (we're not testing scenario engines here).

  Expected:
    - codex-setup creates a bare repo, auto-named
      `verify-fixtures-<writIdTail>`.
    - guild-setup runs `nsg init`, registers the codex from upstream
      yields, no plugins installed.
    - Stub scenario, stub archive, then teardown chain runs.
    - Trial completes; bare repo and test guild dir both gone.

fixtures:
  - id: codex
    engineId: lab.codex-setup
    givens:
      upstreamRepo: /workspace/nexus-mk2
      baseSha: 4b50b6542aa07bd7b74dca1a1d581f90788d4d47

  - id: test-guild
    engineId: lab.guild-setup
    dependsOn: [codex]
    givens:
      # codexes is needed so the test guild's `nsg codex add` command
      # is available for the codex-registration step. link:<path>
      # creates a symlink to the dev source — same shape as vibers'
      # own package.json.
      plugins:
        # tools-apparatus consumes plugin-contributed tool kits.
        # Without it, `nsg codex add` (a tool from codexes-apparatus)
        # is not discoverable as a CLI command.
        - name: '@shardworks/tools-apparatus'
          version: 'file:/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/tools'
        - name: '@shardworks/codexes-apparatus'
          version: 'file:/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/codexes'

scenario:
  engineId: lab.archive  # stub used as a no-op scenario
  givens: {}

probes: []

archive:
  engineId: lab.archive
  givens: {}

97	   * type's state name can round-trip through the book. For mandate-typed
98	   * writs the value is constrained to a `WritPhase` literal at runtime, but
99	   * callers that need a narrower static type should downcast explicitly.
100	   */

103	   * Plugin-owned observation slot (status side of the spec/status split).
104	   *
105	   * The observation slot is a plugin-keyed map (`Record<PluginId,
106	   * unknown>`): each top-level key is a plugin id, and the value is an
107	   * arbitrary shape that plugin publishes for post-hoc observation.
108	   * Ownership is convention-only — plugin `X` writes only to `status[X]`.
109	   *
110	   * There is exactly one sanctioned slot-write path:
111	   * `ClerkApi.setWritStatus(writId, pluginId, value)`, which performs a
112	   * transactional read-modify-write on the sub-slot keyed by `pluginId`
113	   * so sibling sub-slots are preserved under concurrent writers.
114	   * `transition()` silently strips `status` from its body, and the
115	   * generic `put()` / `patch()` paths on the writs book are not
116	   * supported slot-write mechanisms — every route other than
117	   * `setWritStatus()` would wholesale-replace the slot and clobber
118	   * sibling sub-slots. Readers access `writ.status?.[pluginId]` directly.
119	   *
120	   * The slot survives terminal phase transitions.
121	   */
122	  status?: Record<string, unknown>;
123	  /**
124	   * Plugin-owned metadata slot — sibling to `status` but reserved for
125	   * metadata-shape data (provenance, cross-references, classification
126	   * tags, configuration extensions) attached at writ creation or
127	   * registration time, rather than the post-hoc observation that
128	   * `status` records.
129	   *
130	   * The semantic distinction matters: `status` is for what a plugin
131	   * has *observed* about a writ after the fact (a stuck cause, a
132	   * triggering child, a gate result); `ext` is for what a plugin needs
133	   * the writ to *carry* as an attribute of its identity (a petition id,

export interface WritDoc {
  /** Index signature required to satisfy BookEntry constraint. */
  [key: string]: unknown;
  /** Unique writ id (`w-{base36_timestamp}-{hex_random}`). Sortable by creation time. */
  id: string;
  /** Writ type — must be a type declared in guild config, or a built-in type. */
  type: string;
  /**
   * Current lifecycle state name (Clerk-owned; spec side of the spec/status
   * split). Structurally typed as `string` so any plugin-registered writ
   * type's state name can round-trip through the book. For mandate-typed
   * writs the value is constrained to a `WritPhase` literal at runtime, but
   * callers that need a narrower static type should downcast explicitly.
   */
  phase: string;
  /**
   * Plugin-owned observation slot (status side of the spec/status split).
   *
   * The observation slot is a plugin-keyed map (`Record<PluginId,
   * unknown>`): each top-level key is a plugin id, and the value is an
   * arbitrary shape that plugin publishes for post-hoc observation.
   * Ownership is convention-only — plugin `X` writes only to `status[X]`.
   *
   * There is exactly one sanctioned slot-write path:
   * `ClerkApi.setWritStatus(writId, pluginId, value)`, which performs a
   * transactional read-modify-write on the sub-slot keyed by `pluginId`
   * so sibling sub-slots are preserved under concurrent writers.
   * `transition()` silently strips `status` from its body, and the
   * generic `put()` / `patch()` paths on the writs book are not
   * supported slot-write mechanisms — every route other than
   * `setWritStatus()` would wholesale-replace the slot and clobber
   * sibling sub-slots. Readers access `writ.status?.[pluginId]` directly.
   *
   * The slot survives terminal phase transitions.
   */
  status?: Record<string, unknown>;
  /**
   * Plugin-owned metadata slot — sibling to `status` but reserved for
   * metadata-shape data (provenance, cross-references, classification
   * tags, configuration extensions) attached at writ creation or
   * registration time, rather than the post-hoc observation that
   * `status` records.
   *
   * The semantic distinction matters: `status` is for what a plugin
   * has *observed* about a writ after the fact (a stuck cause, a
   * triggering child, a gate result); `ext` is for what a plugin needs
   * the writ to *carry* as an attribute of its identity (a petition id,
   * a classifier tag, a foreign-system reference). Picking the wrong
   * slot layers metadata under an observation contract or vice versa,
   * so plugin authors should choose consciously.
   *
   * Structurally identical to `status`: a plugin-keyed map
   * (`Record<PluginId, unknown>`) where each top-level key is a plugin
   * id and the value is an arbitrary opaque shape. Ownership is
   * convention-only — plugin `X` writes only to `ext[X]`.
   *
   * There is exactly one sanctioned slot-write path:
   * `ClerkApi.setWritExt(writId, pluginId, value)`, which performs a
   * transactional read-modify-write on the sub-slot keyed by `pluginId`
   * so sibling sub-slots are preserved under concurrent writers.
   * `transition()` silently strips `ext` from its body, and the
   * generic `put()` / `patch()` paths on the writs book are not
   * supported slot-write mechanisms — every route other than
   * `setWritExt()` would wholesale-replace the slot and clobber
   * sibling sub-slots. Readers access `writ.ext?.[pluginId]` directly.
   *
   * Optional and absent by default — a freshly-posted writ reads
   * `ext === undefined` until a plugin writes its first sub-slot. The
   * slot survives terminal phase transitions.
   */
  ext?: Record<string, unknown>;
  /** Short human-readable title. */
  title: string;
  /** Detail text. */
  body: string;
  /** Target codex name. */
  codex?: string;
  /** Parent writ id. Absent on root writs. Immutable after creation. */
  parentId?: string;
  /** ISO timestamp when the writ was created. */

cat >> /workspace/nexus-mk2/experiments/data/coco-log.yaml << 'EOF'

- session: 927ab84f-c92d-4a65-94ac-a19daf97ee42
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "Codified pipeline smoke test (concludes c-momaa75l). Located at packages/laboratory/src/integration.test.ts; 7 test cases covering probe → archive → teardown gating → extract end-to-end. Uses memory stacks (StacksTestStub) + a stub Clerk (`show`/`resolveId` only) + a stub Fabricator that exposes the laboratory's engine bag — no Spider, no Animator, no subprocess shell-out, no real `nsg init` or `nsg plugin install`. The synthetic engines we'd ordinarily run via Spider's crawl-loop are run sequentially in dependency order to mirror what Spider would do; engines' run() methods are real, the orchestration is hand-stitched. What's covered: probe-trial-context yields the expected shape (writ snapshot, frameworkVersion, plugin list); archive engine writes one atomic row to lab-trial-archives via buildProbeEntries; teardown engines' tightened safety check refuses-then-allows based on persisted archive row (specifically pinned: codex-teardown no longer requires context.upstream.archive defined, only the persisted row — regression pin for c-momkqtn5); trial-show returns the row via the tool surface; trial-extract dispatches via Fabricator + isProbeEngineDesign type guard, writes manifest.yaml + README.md + per-probe outputs, refuses non-empty target dirs without --force, reports skippedProbes for unregistered engines and engines without extract() handlers. What's NOT covered: real fixture-engine shellouts (codex-setup, guild-setup, commission-post-xguild) which need real subprocess + real test guild on disk; probe-stacks-dump and probe-git-range which need real test guild + real bare repo. Those are exercised by live trials. 167/167 tests passing."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum
EOF

file.content:
# Experimental infrastructure: setup, artifacts, lifecycle

**Status:** draft (2026-04-30) — refined with Sean's feedback.

## Purpose

This document specifies the infrastructure for running framework-side
experiments — runs that need a controlled codex, controlled framework
state, isolated session recording, and reproducible setup. The immediate
driver is P3 (engine-pipeline-decomposition; click `c-modxxtu6`), whose
sub-questions (orientation suppression, trigger heuristics, granularity,
cost confirmation) all require executing real implementer sessions in
isolated conditions before the architecture can be designed empirically.
Subsequent experiments touching framework or commission-pipeline behavior
will reuse the same infrastructure.

The infrastructure does **not** govern paper analyses (instruments run
against existing transcript archives) — those need no setup. It governs
work that **executes new commissions** under controlled conditions.

## Vocabulary

- **Experiment.** One piece of inquiry — a research question plus the
  analytical frame to answer it. Lives at
  `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/`.
- **Run.** One execution of the experimental apparatus with a specific
  set of variable settings. An experiment produces one or more runs.
  Lives at `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/`.
- **Variables.** The inputs that define a run. Split into **controlled**
  (held constant — framework SHA, codex base, common config) and
  **manipulated** (varied per run — prompts, plugin versions, config
  fragments). Both kinds live in `run.yaml`.

## Design principles

1. **Three-repo separation.** The sanctum, the framework (nexus), and the
   codex live in three repos with distinct persistence rules. Each artifact
   has one canonical home.
2. **One guild per run.** Every run gets its own fresh guild and its
   own short-lived codex repo. This eliminates run-isolation problems

1	# The {Name} — API Contract
2	
3	Status: **Draft**
4	
5	Package: `@shardworks/{package-name}` · Plugin id: `{plugin-id}`
6	
7	> **⚠️ MVP scope.** {One-paragraph summary of what's included in MVP and what's deferred. Remove this callout if the spec covers the full design.}

1	# The Fabricator — API Contract
2	
3	Status: **Draft — MVP**
4	
5	Package: `@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus` · Plugin id: `fabricator`
6	
7	> **⚠️ MVP scope.** The first implementation is an engine design registry with kit scanning and a single lookup method. No capability resolution, no need-based queries, no chain composition. The Fabricator earns those features when dynamic rig extension arrives.
8	
9	---
10	
11	## Purpose
12	
13	The Fabricator is the guild's capability catalog. It holds engine design specifications and serves them to the Spider on demand. When the Spider needs to run an engine, it asks the Fabricator for the design by ID — the Fabricator resolves it, the Spider runs it.
14	
15	The Fabricator does **not** execute engines. It does not touch rigs, manage sessions, or interact with the Clerk. It is a pure query service: designs in, designs out.
16	
17	---
18	
19	## Dependencies
20	
21	```
22	requires: []
23	consumes: ['engines']    — scans kit and supportKit contributions for engine designs
24	```
25	
26	---
27	
28	## Engine Design Contract
29	
30	The `@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus` package is the canonical home for the `EngineDesign` interface. Kit authors and apparatus that contribute engines import from this package:
31	
32	```typescript
33	import type { EngineDesign, EngineRunContext, EngineRunResult } from '@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus'
34	```
35	
36	### `EngineDesign`
37	
38	```typescript
39	interface EngineDesign {
40	  /** Unique identifier for this engine design (e.g. 'draft', 'implement', 'review'). */
41	  id: string
42	
43	  /**
44	   * Execute this engine.
45	   *
46	   * Returns 'completed' with yields (synchronous work done inline), or
47	   * 'launched' with a sessionId (async work the Spider polls for).
48	   * The Spider inspects the result shape — no need to declare the kind up front.
49	   *
50	   * @param givens — the engine's declared inputs, assembled by the Spider.
51	   *   A mix of values from the givensSpec (set at rig spawn time, e.g. role,
52	   *   buildCommand, writ) and upstream yields (resolved from completed engines,
53	   *   e.g. draft worktree path). The engine doesn't know or care about
54	   *   provenance — all values arrive the same way.
55	   *
56	   * @param context — minimal execution context. The engine id and an escape hatch
57	   *   (`upstream`) containing all upstream yields keyed by engine id.
58	   *
59	   * Engines pull their own apparatus dependencies via guild().apparatus(...) —
60	   * same pattern as tool handlers.
61	   */
62	  run(givens: Record<string, unknown>, context: EngineRunContext): Promise<EngineRunResult>
63	
64	  /**
65	   * Assemble yields from a completed session.
66	   *
67	   * Called by the Spider's collect step when a quick engine's session
68	   * reaches a terminal state. The engine looks up whatever it needs
69	   * via guild() — same dependency pattern as run().
70	   *
71	   * @param sessionId — the session to collect yields from (primary input).
72	   * @param givens    — same givens that were passed to run().
73	   * @param context   — same execution context that was passed to run().
74	   *
75	   * If not defined, the Spider uses a generic default:
76	   *   { sessionId, sessionStatus, output? }
77	   *
78	   * Only relevant for quick engines (those that return { status: 'launched' }).
79	   * Clockwork engines return yields directly from run().
80	   */
81	  collect?(sessionId: string, givens: Record<string, unknown>, context: EngineRunContext): Promise<unknown>
82	}
83	```
84	
85	### `EngineRunContext`
86	
87	```typescript
88	interface EngineRunContext {
89	  /** Simple string identity for this engine instance (e.g. 'draft', 'implement'). */
90	  engineId: string
91	  /** All upstream yields, keyed by engine id. Escape hatch for engines that need to inspect the full upstream chain. */
92	  upstream: Record<string, unknown>
93	}
94	```
95	
96	### `EngineRunResult`
97	
98	```typescript
99	type EngineRunResult =
100	  | { status: 'completed'; yields: unknown }    // clockwork: done, here are the yields
101	  | { status: 'launched'; sessionId: string }    // quick: session launched, Spider will poll
102	```
103	
104	---
105	
106	## Kit Contribution
107	
108	Kits contribute engine designs via an `engines` field:
109	
110	```typescript
111	export default {
112	  kit: {
113	    requires: ['fabricator'],
114	    engines: {
115	      draft:     draftEngine,
116	      implement: implementEngine,
117	      review:    reviewEngine,
118	    },
119	  },
120	} satisfies Plugin
121	```
122	
123	Each value is an `EngineDesign`. The Fabricator scans these contributions at startup via `ctx.kits()` and reactively via the `apparatus:started` event — the same pattern the Instrumentarium uses for tools. See the [Instrumentarium spec](instrumentarium.md) for the reference implementation of kit-contribution scanning.
124	
125	---
126	
127	## Support Kit
128	
129	None. No books, no tools. The Fabricator is a pure in-memory registry.
130	
131	---
132	
133	## `FabricatorApi` Interface (`provides`)
134	
135	```typescript
136	interface FabricatorApi {
137	  /**
138	   * Look up an engine design by ID.
139	   * Returns the design if registered, undefined otherwise.
140	   */
141	  getEngineDesign(id: string): EngineDesign | undefined
142	}
143	```
144	
145	---
146	
147	## Configuration
148	
149	None. No `guild.json` entry needed. Note: the per-design `retry` policy declared on `EngineDesign` can be overridden at the deployment layer via Spider's `spider.engineRetryOverrides` config block — see `spider.md` for the full shape, validation rules, and example. The Fabricator itself stays config-blind; the override-aware resolver `resolveEngineRetryConfigWithOverrides` takes the override map as a parameter.
150	
151	---
152	
153	## Implementation Notes
154	
155	- The implementation is small: a `Map<string, EngineDesign>` populated by scanning kit contributions at startup, and a single `get` method. Ship it as a standalone package (`@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus`) to establish the dependency boundary and to own the `EngineDesign` type exports.
156	- The Instrumentarium's kit-scanning lifecycle is the model to follow — eager reading of `ctx.kits()` at start time plus reactive consumption of `apparatus:started` events, collecting contributions into an internal registry.
157	
158	---
159	
160	## Future
161	
162	The Fabricator is deliberately thin. Its planned evolution:
163	
164	**Capability resolution.** When dynamic rig extension arrives, the API grows:
165	
166	```typescript
167	interface FabricatorApi {
168	  // ... existing methods ...
169	
170	  /**
171	   * Resolve a declared need to an engine chain.
172	   * Searches installed engine designs for those that satisfy the need,
173	   * composes them into an ordered chain, and returns the chain for the
174	   * Spider to graft onto the rig.
175	   */
176	  resolve(need: string, context?: ResolutionContext): EngineChain | null
177	}
178	```
179	
180	The Fabricator is also the Sage's entry point: planning animas query it to introspect what the guild can build before decomposing a commission into writs. A standalone Fabricator (rather than capability resolution buried inside the Spider) is what makes this possible — it's a shared service both the Spider and the Sage can call.
181	
182	**Unified capability catalog.** The Fabricator may absorb tool designs from the Instrumentarium, becoming the single answer to "what can this guild do?" regardless of whether the answer is an engine or a tool.
183
