# Experiments Index

Maintained by Coco. Update when experiments are created, activated, or closed.

## Active

| # | Name | Research Question |
|---|---|---|
| [X006](X006-guild-metaphor/spec.md) | The Guild Metaphor | Does the guild metaphor improve agent performance and patron experience compared to plain instructions? |
| [X008](X008-patrons-hands/spec.md) | The Patron's Hands | How does the bootstrap period (Coco doing implementation) shape patron expectations when autonomous agents come online? |
| [X010](X010-staged-sessions/spec.md) | Staged Sessions | What is the relationship between session length, cost, and output quality for autonomous commissions? Phase 1 (cost curve) complete at original scale; Apr 29 addendum sharpens H4 into H4a/H4b/H4c sub-hypotheses (cost model + handoff thresholds) and adds H5 (inventory pure-read bloat). |
| [X011](X011-context-debt/spec.md) | Context Debt | How much context window is consumed by tool output never referenced again, and can we reduce it? Activated 2026-04-29 with the read-utilization instrument as its first artifact. |

## Ready (approved, not yet started)

| # | Name | Research Question |
|---|---|---|
| [X003](X003-commission-prompt-tuning/spec.md) | Commission Prompt Tuning | How do pre-commission instructions affect agent performance on identical tasks? |
| [X004](X004-iteration-context/spec.md) | Iteration Context | How does context about prior sessions affect quality and safety when an agent iterates on its own work? |
| [X009](X009-metaphor-as-instruction/spec.md) | Metaphor as Implicit Instruction | Does the guild metaphor carry actual behavioral weight, or is it decorative? |

## Draft (not yet approved)

| # | Name | Research Question |
|---|---|---|
| [X005](X005-greenfield-assembly/spec.md) | Greenfield Assembly | What if agents never iterate on existing code — only build net-new? |
| [X012](X012-agent-oriented-code/spec.md) | Agent-Oriented Code | Do conventional clean code principles still apply when agents are the primary authors? |
| [X014](X014-technical-spec-quality/spec.md) | Technical Spec Quality | Do technically detailed specs outperform mountain-quality specs — and does it depend on complexity? |
| [X015](X015-spec-detail-model-substitute/spec.md) | Spec Detail as Model Substitute | Can planning-pipeline specs reduce the model capability threshold, enabling Sonnet-class models to match Opus outcomes? |

content.text:
Everything is committed. Here's the summary:

## Lab implementation — work completed unattended

**Commits landed (sanctum):**
- `e548c5ec` — three probes + archive engine + tightened teardown safety check (1761 insertions)
- `0274b3f8` — three retrieval CLI tools (trial-show, trial-extract, trial-export-book) + probe/archive unit tests (853 insertions)
- `befd3562` — codified pipeline smoke test (487 insertions)
- `a85804a4` — README authoring guide flesh-out + infra-spec retirement (197 insertions)

**Commits landed (nexus):**
- `262f8dd` — apparatus architecture doc at `docs/architecture/apparatus/laboratory.md` + opt-in apparatus table entry. Not pushed (per directive — no push without explicit ask).

**Tests:** 167/167 passing. Typecheck clean. No remaining stubs.

**Clicks concluded:** `c-molveg89`, `c-momkil4p`, `c-momaa3w7`, `c-momkqtn5`, `c-momaa75l`, `c-momaa8mk`, `c-momaaa3t`. Only `c-momaab8y` (first real-world trial port) remains live — explicitly deferred per handoff.

**Two grit clicks opened** under the impl parent for follow-up: `c-momm4abc` (failed-trial orphan cleanup) and `c-momm4i15` (daemon ↔ CLI Scriptorium state drift).

## Decisions made unattended (review)

1. **Probe registry replaced with type-guard pattern** — per your mid-session note. `ProbeEngineDesign` extends `EngineDesign` with `extract()`; `lab-trial-extract` resolves via `FabricatorApi.getEngineDesign(id) + isProbeEngineDesign(design)`. No separate registry. The handoff text was the previous design (`registerProbe()` calls); the type-guard approach is cleaner and earlier code was discarded after your note.

2. **`probe-trial-context.frameworkVersion` is `guildConfig.nexus`, not a git SHA.** The apparatus runs out of an npm-installed package; a literal git SHA would require knowing it's running from a checkout. The version string is the portable identifier; deeper provenance can be added later via a configurable given. (Documented in the engine docstring.)

3. **`probe-stacks-dump` source-book naming.** Prefix-match against the test guild's `guildConfig.plugins` to recover `<owner>/<book>` form when resolvable; falls back to the raw `books_…` table name. No SQL-side metadata for owner-id, so reverse-engineering the table name is heuristic. Documented in code; longer plugin ids win on ambiguity (`clockworks-stacks-signals` beats `clockworks` when both could match).

4. **10MB per-diff cap is a hard fail-loud tripwire**, not a soft warning. Failing the whole probe (and thus the trial) makes the surprise visible; designing blob storage was tagged as future work if it ever bites.

5. **`trial-extract` refuses non-empty target dirs without `--force`.** Defensive default; chose this over silent-overwrite to make accidents recoverable.

6. **`trial-export-book` defaults to JSONL**, not JSON. Typical consumer is a streaming analysis pipeline; JSON mode is opt-in.

7. **Skipped a separate sanctum-side user guide.** The README's expanded "Authoring trials" section covers end-user authoring; the sanctum's `docs/` tree has no obvious user-guide section yet. Reopen if a gap surfaces after the first real trial port.

8. **Infra spec archived rather than deleted.** Moved to `docs/archive/deprecated-docs/experimental-infrastructure-setup-and-artifacts.md` with a forward-pointer header. Captures the design thinking that preceded the apparatus — useful context for anyone reading the apparatus later.

9. **Smoke test scope: synthetic-engine pipeline, not full Spider stack.** Uses memory stacks + stub Clerk + stub Fabricator that exposes the lab engine bag; engines run sequentially in dep order to mirror Spider crawl. Deliberately out of scope: subprocess fixture engines (codex-setup, guild-setup, commission-post-xguild) and probe engines that need a real test guild on disk (probe-stacks-dump, probe-git-range) — those are exercised by live verifies and the X016 port.

10. **Test stub Clerk is a partial implementation** — only `show` and `resolveId` are real; other methods throw "not implemented" so any future test code that reaches for them surfaces the gap rather than silently no-op. Same for Fabricator (real `getEngineDesign` against the lab engine bag).

## What requires you (live verification, not workable unattended)

- **Restart vibers' daemon** to pick up the new engine + book registrations; the current daemon predates everything in this session.
- **Live-verify the four-tool `nsg lab` auto-grouping** activates with all four subcommands.
- **Post a synthetic trial against vibers** with the full probe set (trial-context + stacks-dump + git-range) and inspect the resulting archive row + extracted directory structure. The codified smoke test covers the orchestration layer; live verification is needed for the SQLite-attaching probes and the codex-bare git walk.
- **Three orphan codexes in vibers** (`verify-fixtures-{1267530e,2244ce82,40dd2663}`) will only clear on daemon restart — known issue per the new `c-momm4i15` click.
- **Port the first real-world trial (X016 orientation suppression or similar P3 candidate)** — the capstone item per click `c-momaab8y`. Likely surfaces missed assumptions in the archive layout or probe outputs that the synthetic smoke test won't catch; expect a small iteration loop on first run.

message.content:
regarding shellling to "nsg"... it occurs to me that this tool won't always be installed globally. we need to make sure that it is invoking this tool via the "temp guild's" install, through an npx-type equivalent, or maybe just incoming the bin directly. probably interesting landmines around install versions here ..

lmk on this. still reading your earlier decision list to provide feedback

content.text:
Right, this is a real landmine. Let me think through the design space.

## The current state

Three engines shell out to `nsg`:

- **`lab.guild-setup`** → `nsg init`, `nsg --guild-root <X> plugin install`, `nsg --guild-root <X> codex add`
- **`lab.commission-post-xguild`** → `nsg --guild-root <X> commission-post`, `nsg --guild-root <X> writ-show`
- **`lab.codex-fixture`** does NOT shell to `nsg` (uses Scriptorium API directly)

All three use **bare `nsg`** — relying on PATH lookup. That works in your dev container because of `/usr/local/bin/nsg` → source wrapper, but it's brittle in three independent ways:

1. **No global `nsg`** — production install of the laboratory plugin lands a user with a guild but maybe no global `nsg` (or a different version). The engines fail with `command not found`.
2. **Wrong-version global `nsg`** — user has `nsg` v0.5 globally, but the lab guild was built against v0.7. The CLI surface drifted; trial fails opaquely.
3. **Path-shadowing** — anything earlier on PATH masks the framework's `nsg`. Dev environments have layers of wrappers; production has corporate base images.

## What `nsg` actually has to be

For *every* shellout in the lab:

- The CLI tool is operating against a **target guild** (lab-host or test guild) via `--guild-root`. It reads that guild's `guild.json`, loads plugins from that guild's `node_modules`, and dispatches.
- The `nsg` *binary* itself needs to be **compatible** with the target guild's framework version (the `nexus` field in `guild.json`).
- In MVP we can assume "same major version family" is enough. The interesting compatibility question (what happens when lab-host is at v0.7 and test guild's `nsg init` writes a guild that thinks it's v0.7) is a separate problem.

So the question reduces to: **which `nsg` binary do we invoke?**

## Options

### A. Resolve from the lab-host's local install

The lab guild has `@shardworks/laboratory-apparatus` installed. Make laboratory peerDep on the CLI package (`@shardworks/nexus-cli` or whatever the package id is) so it's guaranteed in the lab-host's `node_modules`. Then resolve the binary at engine time:

```ts
// Once at module init:
const nsgBin = require.resolve('@shardworks/nexus-cli/bin/nsg');
// or, more portable across cjs/esm + dev/prod:
const cliEntry = require.resolve('@shardworks/nexus-cli/dist/cli.js');

// At engine run:
await execFile('node', [cliEntry, ...args], { cwd, env });
```

**Pros**:
- Hermetic. The `nsg` we shell to is the version the lab-host operator pinned via npm. No PATH dependency, no global install required.
- Works the same in dev and prod (assuming the CLI package's `package.json` has the right `bin` / `main` entries).
- "Whichever `nsg` lab-host uses to commission *itself*" is the same `nsg` it uses for fixture work — there's a single declared version per lab guild.
- Plays nicely with our existing peerDeps story.

**Cons**:
- Adds a peerDep we didn't have before. (Currently the CLI is invoked entirely externally — the laboratory has no compile-time reference to it.)
- If the framework decides to ship the CLI as a separate npm package vs. bundling it into core, the import path changes.
- In your dev container, `nsg` invokes TS source via `--experimental-transform-types`. We'd need the same Node flags when we `execFile('node', [cliEntry, ...])` — currently the wrapper sets them; we'd need to either find that wrapper or replicate the flag in the spawn.

### B. Path-resolve via the lab-host's `node_modules/.bin/`

```ts
const nsgBin = path.join(guild().home, 'node_modules', '.bin', 'nsg');
if (!existsSync(nsgBin)) {
  throw new Error(
    `[lab.guild-setup] no local nsg at ${nsgBin}; ensure @shardworks/nexus-cli is ` +
    `installed in the lab guild (peerDep of @shardworks/laboratory).`
  );
}
await execFile(nsgBin, args, { cwd, env });
```

**Pros**:
- Zero TypeScript-level coupling to the CLI package. The laboratory just looks for a binstub at a conventional path.
- Whatever `nsg` lab-host has, we use. Same dev-vs-prod consistency.
- `node_modules/.bin/nsg` is npm/pnpm's standard binstub location — already there if any package in the lab-host's tree has `bin: { nsg: ... }`.

**Cons**:
- Hard-codes the binstub layout. If the lab-host is bootstrapped by some package manager that doesn't materialize `.bin/` (rare), we miss it.
- The dev wrapper at `/usr/local/bin/nsg` *might* not match this path (your dev is running from `/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/cli.ts` — there's no `node_modules/.bin/nsg` in `/workspace/vibers/`, presumably).

### C. Configurable, with sensible default

Add a lab-guild config option (`laboratory.nsgBinPath` or env `LAB_NSG_BIN`) that defaults to option A or B's resolution. Makes dev-mode overrides explicit:

```yaml
# Lab guild's guild.json
laboratory:
  nsgBinPath: /usr/local/bin/nsg   # opt-in override; default is resolved
```

**Pros**:
- Production and dev both work; dev users with custom wrappers can point at them.
- Doesn't lock us into one resolution strategy.

**Cons**:
- Config surface area. Now there's a knob to misconfigure.
- "Default is resolved" still needs to pick A or B.

### D. Drop the shell-out entirely

The cross-guild scenario engine particularly bothers me. We're shelling to `nsg --guild-root <X> commission-post` to land a writ in another guild. We could instead — programmatically — open the test guild via `createGuild({ home })`, wait for `phase:started`, call `clerk.post(...)` directly, and shut down. Same effect, no subprocess.

For `nsg init`, this works too: the CLI calls some `init()` library function that actually creates the guild. We can call that directly from the engine.

For `plugin install`, this is harder — it's a wrapper around `npm install`, which has its own subprocess concerns.

**Pros**:
- Zero CLI coupling. Engines just import library functions and call them.
- Faster (no node startup per call).
- Type-checked.

**Cons**:
- Heavy lift. `commission-post-xguild` becomes "open a fresh guild handle, run commission-post's logic, close" — a non-trivial library API surface that doesn't currently exist.
- Plugin install still has to shell out to `npm`, so we're not fully shell-free.
- This is a v2 architecture (the parked `c-mom9vm3n` cross-guild engine surface).

## My recommendation

**Option B** for MVP: resolve `<labHost>/node_modules/.bin/nsg`, fail loud if missing, and document the peerDep on `@shardworks/nexus-cli` (or whatever the package id ends up being).

Reasoning:
- Smallest change to the existing engines (replace the bare `'nsg'` string with a resolved path).
- Doesn't require importing the CLI package in TypeScript — keeps the laboratory's compile-time dependency surface clean.
- Catches the "no local `nsg`" case at engine start with a clear error message rather than letting `command not found` propagate.
- Works the same in dev and prod once the lab-host actually has the CLI installed locally (which it should, since you're scaffolding labs as guilds and presumably the standard guild has the CLI in its install set).

The path I'd land on:

```ts
// New helper in engines/nsg-bin.ts:
import { existsSync } from 'node:fs';
import path from 'node:path';
import { guild } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';

let cached: string | null = null;
export function resolveNsgBin(): string {
  if (cached) return cached;
  const labHost = guild().home;
  const candidate = path.join(labHost, 'node_modules', '.bin', 'nsg');
  if (!existsSync(candidate)) {
    throw new Error(
      `[laboratory] no local nsg binstub at ${candidate}. The lab guild must have ` +
      `@shardworks/nexus-cli installed (peerDep of @shardworks/laboratory). ` +
      `If you're running in dev mode with a non-standard install layout, set ` +
      `LAB_NSG_BIN to override.`
    );
  }
  cached = process.env.LAB_NSG_BIN ?? candidate;
  return cached;
}
```

And replace every `exec('nsg', ['--guild-root', X, ...])` with `exec(resolveNsgBin(), ['--guild-root', X, ...])`. Three engines, ~6-8 call sites.

The escape-hatch env var (`LAB_NSG_BIN`) handles your dev container where the wrapper is at `/usr/local/bin/nsg` and `vibers/node_modules/.bin/nsg` may or may not exist.

## Things I'd flag as still-open

- **Does the standard guild install `@shardworks/nexus-cli` locally?** If not, this whole approach fails and we need option A (require.resolve from the laboratory plugin's deps, which we can guarantee). I don't actually know — your dev container hides this because the global wrapper works.
- **Does the test guild also need a local `nsg`?** No — under this proposal we always shell to the lab-host's `nsg --guild-root <test-guild>`. The test guild's `node_modules` only needs the plugins, not the CLI.
- **What about version drift?** This MVP punts on it. If lab-host is on v0.7 and a trial pins the test guild's nexus version to v0.5, the lab-host's v0.7 `nsg` is talking to a v0.5 guild. That'll work or not depending on CLI/guild compatibility — same problem any cross-version `nsg` invocation has, not unique to the laboratory.
- **Option D (drop the shell-out) is the right v2 answer.** We should track it as a parked successor click, but it's the wrong scope to attempt mid-MVP.

Want me to land Option B as a follow-up commit, or wait for your read-through and broader feedback?

message.content:
Option B could work. I wanna propose two additional options to weight against it:

- Use `npx` to invoke a specific version of the CLI every time it is needed. Probably very slow, because it will install for each plugin, etc.
- Always install `@shardworks/nexus-cli` into the _test guild_, and run it from there. The version of the CLI to use can be part of the trial metadata. If it's not part of the input when the trial is created it can be populated from the 'lab host' guild's version, or the declared peer dep version of hte lab plugin

content.text:
Both worth thinking through. Let me go through them.

## Option E — `npx` per invocation

I'd put this strictly worse than B for our case:

- **Slow.** First-run installs to `~/.npm/_npx/<hash>/` — that's a full npm resolve + install of `@shardworks/nexus-cli` and its deps (including better-sqlite3, which has a native build step). Subsequent runs hit the cache but still pay node startup + npx overhead. Our 11-engine trials are already ~14s wallclock; adding a per-call npx cost balloons that.
- **Network coupled.** `npx <pkg>` does a registry probe for "is this the latest" unless you carefully construct the args. CI without network — broken.
- **Hard to point at local source.** In dev, you want to test framework changes against trials. `npx file:<path>` works but is awkward, and you've still got the cold-install cost on first invocation per session.
- **Cache pollution.** Each distinct version pin lands a directory. Not catastrophic but cruft I'd rather avoid.

The only thing npx buys us that B doesn't is "version pinning per call" — and that's exactly what your Option F gives us with substantially better ergonomics.

## Option F — install `@shardworks/nexus-cli` into the test guild

This is meaningfully better than B on the dimension I think you're poking at: **trial reproducibility**. The laboratory's whole pitch is "trials are reproducible" — we capture rig template, framework version, plugin pins, manifest. If the *framework version that ran the trial* is just "whatever the lab-host happens to have," we're capturing it as observation, not as input. Trial reproducibility means you can re-author the manifest a year later and get the same versions back; that's only true if the framework version is on the manifest, not on the lab-host's install.

Comparison vs B on the dimensions that matter:

| Concern | B (lab-host's local nsg) | F (test guild's pinned nsg) |
|---|---|---|
| Per-trial version pin | No — tied to whatever the lab-host has | **Yes — manifest declares it** |
| Cross-version risk | Real (lab-host's CLI driving a test guild on a different `nexus`) | **Eliminated by construction** |
| Probe-trial-context capture | Observation only | **Authoritative** (input → output) |
| Dev iteration on framework | Wrapper masks it (works), but trial reproducibility suffers | Clean: `cliVersion: file:/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli` in manifest, build before trial |
| Code complexity | ~5 lines | More — bootstrap chicken-and-egg + manifest schema field |
| Code we'd own | Just resolution helper | Bootstrap sequencing + manifest field + version-defaulting logic |

### The bootstrap problem (and how I'd handle it)

`nsg init <path>` has to come from *some* `nsg`. The test guild doesn't have one yet. So Option F is necessarily hybrid:

```
PHASE 1 — bootstrap (uses lab-host's nsg, à la Option B):
  1. lab-host nsg init <testGuild>
  2. lab-host nsg --guild-root <X> plugin install @shardworks/nexus-cli@<pinned>
  3. (optional) patch <X>/guild.json `nexus` field to <pinned>

PHASE 2 — runtime (uses test guild's pinned nsg):
  4. <testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg --guild-root <X> plugin install <trial plugins...>
  5. <testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg --guild-root <X> codex add ...
  6. <testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg --guild-root <X> commission-post ...
  7. <testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg --guild-root <X> writ-show ...
```

So the lab-host's `nsg` reduces to **two** invocations, and only those two have to handle "lab-host CLI driving a possibly-different-version guild." `nsg init` and the first plugin install are pretty stable across CLI versions; the version-sensitive operations (which read full guild config and load plugins) all happen under the pinned CLI.

### Where the version comes from

Cascade, in order:
1. **Manifest's `frameworkVersion`** (or `cliVersion` — name TBD) if explicitly declared.
2. **Lab-host's installed `@shardworks/nexus-cli` version** as default. We can read this at engine time from `<labHost>/node_modules/@shardworks/nexus-cli/package.json`.
3. **Laboratory plugin's peerDep declaration** as the floor. (Less likely to be the right default than #2 but useful as a sanity check.)

Probe-trial-context already captures `frameworkVersion`; under this proposal it'd capture the **resolved** version (whatever was actually installed) into the trial's archive row. The manifest snapshot in the same probe captures the **declared** value. Both are visible to analysis.

### One subtle gotcha

In your dev container, `nsg` is `node --experimental-transform-types <path>/cli.ts`. If we install `@shardworks/nexus-cli` via `file:/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli`, npm copies the package contents (or symlinks) and the bin entry runs whatever the package.json's `bin` field points at — likely `./dist/cli.js`. So in dev, **trials would only see source changes if the framework is built first.** That's actually correct behavior for a trial apparatus (trials test built artifacts, not WIP TS source) but it's a real change to the dev rhythm. Worth being explicit about.

Mitigations:
- Document the build-before-trial rhythm in the dev workflow doc.
- Optional: `cliVersion: 'dev-source'` magic value that points at the wrapper directly (`/usr/local/bin/nsg`) for explicit live-source iteration. Skips the install step, accepts the hermeticity tradeoff.

## My recommendation

I think we should go with **Option F**, with two notes:

1. Keep Phase 1 (bootstrap) using the lab-host's local `nsg` per Option B's resolution. Two shellouts that need lab-host CLI is a small enough surface to live with, and it eliminates the bootstrap chicken-and-egg cleanly.
2. Add `cliVersion` to the manifest schema as optional, with the cascade above. Default-from-lab-host means existing manifests keep working without changes; explicit trials can pin.

The reproducibility argument carries it for me. The whole point of the apparatus's "DB-authoritative archive" + "manifest as the input record" is that *trials are inputs you can re-run later*. If the framework version is observable but not specifiable, that's a hole.

The implementation is bigger than B but not by much:

- Add `cliVersion?: string` to `LaboratoryTrialConfig` and the manifest schema.
- In `lab.guild-setup`:
  - Resolve effective version (manifest → lab-host package.json → laboratory peerDep).
  - Phase 1 calls (`nsg init`, plugin-install of nexus-cli) use the lab-host's `node_modules/.bin/nsg` (Option B's helper).
  - Optional: post-init, patch `guild.json`'s `nexus` field to the resolved version.
  - All subsequent calls (codex add, the rest of the plugin-install loop, plus everything in the scenario engine) use `<testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg`.
- In `lab.commission-post-xguild` and `lab.wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild`: discover the test guild path from upstream (already do this), then resolve `<testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg`.
- Plugin pins yielded from `lab.guild-setup` (`pluginsResolved`) start including `@shardworks/nexus-cli@<resolved>` so the trial-context probe captures it.

I'd estimate ~2-3 hours of focused work plus tests. Want me to land it as a follow-up commit (separate from the current branch state) so it's easy to review and revert if the bootstrap behavior surprises us?

file.content:
- **`fixtures[].teardownEngineId`**: optional override; defaults to
  the convention `<engineId-with-trailing -setup→-teardown>`.
- **Givens**: opaque to the manifest CLI; each engine's docstring
  documents what it expects. Mistyped givens fail at engine run
  time, not validation.

### After posting

```sh
# Post the trial; the rig fires immediately (writ goes new → open).
nsg lab trial-post manifest.yaml

# Watch the rig flow.
nsg writ list --type trial
nsg writ-show <trialId>           # writ-level view
nsg rig list --writ <trialId>     # the rig executing it

# After the trial completes:
nsg lab trial-show <trialId>      # archive metadata + probe summaries
nsg lab trial-extract <trialId> --to /tmp/extract  # materialize captured data
nsg lab trial-export-book <trialId> --book animator/sessions  # streaming JSONL
```

### Authoring tips

- **Codex base SHA picks the snapshot.** The codex-setup engine
  clones the upstream repo, checks out `baseSha`, and pushes that
  to the trial's local-bare repo. Pick the SHA you want the test
  guild to start from — typically the head of whatever branch
  embodies the variant under test.
- **Plugin pins are passed through to npm.** `file:<absolute-path>`
  is the canonical way to point at local source (the path becomes
  a tarball-equivalent npm install). Use git+URL#<sha> to pin to a
  specific upstream commit on a public repo. Avoid `link:` —
  pnpm-only.

input.new_string:
- **`fixtures[].teardownEngineId`**: optional override; defaults to
  the convention `<engineId-with-trailing -setup→-teardown>`.
- **Givens**: opaque to the manifest CLI for general shape; each
  engine's docstring documents what it expects. Mistyped givens fail
  at engine run time, not validation. **Exception: plugin pins are
  validated at manifest-load time** — see "Plugin pin reproducibility"
  below.

### Plugin pin reproducibility

A trial manifest is a reproducibility artifact: it lands on the
trial writ's `ext.laboratory.config`, gets snapshotted into the
archive row by `lab.probe-trial-context`, and must re-resolve to
the same artifacts when re-run later. The manifest CLI rejects any
plugin pin that doesn't resolve to a stable identifier.

**Accepted forms (whitelist):**

| Form | Example |
|---|---|
| Exact npm semver | `1.2.3`, `0.7.0-alpha.2`, `1.0.0+build.5` |
| Git URL with a SHA fragment | `git+https://github.com/foo/bar.git#a1b2c3d4e5f6...` |
| Git URL via local-file scheme + SHA | `git+file:///workspace/nexus-mk2#a1b2c3d4...` |
| GitHub shorthand with SHA | `foo/bar#a1b2c3d` or `github:foo/bar#a1b2c3d` |
| Registry tarball URL | `https://registry.npmjs.org/foo/-/foo-1.2.3.tgz` |

**Rejected forms (blacklist):**

`file:<path>`, `link:<path>`, `workspace:*`, version ranges
(`^1.2.3`, `~1.2.3`, `*`, `>=1.0.0`), dist-tags (`latest`, `next`,
`beta`, `alpha`, `canary`, `rc`), git URLs with a branch or tag
fragment (`...#main`, `...#v1.0.0`), and partial / unrecognized
specifiers.

**Dev iteration on framework source.** When you want to test a
manifest against in-flight framework changes, commit the changes
locally and pin via the local-file git URL form:

```yaml
plugins:
  - name: '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus'
    version: 'git+file:///workspace/nexus#a1b2c3d4...'
```

The SHA pins the artifact deterministically; the URL just tells the
resolver where to fetch from. No need to push to the remote.

The validator runs in `lab-trial-post`'s manifest-load step. Failed
pins surface with the issue path (e.g.
`fixtures.0.givens.plugins.2.version`) and a specific reason for
each rejection — multiple bad pins in one manifest are reported
together.

content.text:
Done. Summary of what landed in this commit:

## Stable-pin enforcement

- **`src/stable-pin.ts`** — `isStablePin(spec)` predicate, whitelist-first (exact semver, `git+<scheme>://...#<sha>` for any scheme including `git+file://`, GitHub shorthand `foo/bar#<sha>`, registry tarball URLs). Blacklist with specific rejection reasons: `file:`, `link:`, `workspace:`, version ranges, dist-tags, git URLs with branch/tag fragments. Strict.
- **`manifest.ts` superRefine** — uses a `PIN_EXTRACTORS` map keyed by engineId. Currently only `lab.guild-setup` is registered (it's the only engine that takes plugin pins). When new engines like `cliVersion`-bearing scenarios land, they register their extractor in the same place.
- **Errors surface at the issue path** (e.g. `fixtures.0.givens.plugins.2.version`) with a specific reason. Multiple bad pins in one manifest are reported together rather than one-at-a-time.
- **Dev iteration intact** — `git+file:///path/to/repo#<sha>` is accepted. Devs commit locally and pin to that SHA without needing to push.
- 34 new tests; 201/201 passing.

## What's still open from the bigger conversation

- **Option F (test guild's pinned `nsg`).** Approved in principle but not implemented. The plumbing change is independent of the validation that just landed (validation works for both B and F). Want me to land Option F as a follow-up, or wait for separate confirmation?
- **`cliVersion` field on the manifest.** Part of Option F; would also flow through the new `isStablePin` validator (just add another entry to `PIN_EXTRACTORS` or a top-level extractor for the field).
- **Earlier decision-list items 1-11** — you mentioned you were still reading those when the `nsg`-shellout topic came up. Happy to wait on those before moving further.

content.text:
OK, now I have the full picture. Two findings change the calculus:

1. **The CLI package is `@shardworks/nexus`** (not `nexus-cli`). The `nsg init` command is defined as a `ToolDefinition` exported via `frameworkCommands` from that package's public surface.

2. **`nsg init`'s implementation has a dev-guard** (line 58 of `init.ts`): `if (VERSION !== '0.0.0') { dependencies['@shardworks/nexus'] = '^${VERSION}'; ... npm install }`. When the framework is running from unbuilt source (VERSION='0.0.0'), init **skips writing the framework dep AND skips npm install**. The wrapper at `/usr/local/bin/nsg` gives the dev experience by routing to global tooling instead.

This affects both options. Let me re-frame:

## The three real bootstrap options

### G1 — Import `init` from the laboratory's resolved CLI package

```ts
import { frameworkCommands } from '@shardworks/nexus';
const initTool = frameworkCommands.find((t) => t.name === 'init');
await initTool.handler({ path: testGuildPath, name, model });
```

- **Version**: whatever the laboratory's installed `@shardworks/nexus-core` says — i.e., the lab-host's version. Not the trial's pinned version.
- **Dev-guard fires**: in dev (lab-host source mode), VERSION='0.0.0', init skips the framework dep + npm install. We'd have to detect that and patch package.json + run npm install ourselves anyway. So G1 partially-reimplements G2 in dev mode.
- **Coupling**: depends on `frameworkCommands` staying public. It is, but we'd be calling a tool's handler outside of any tool dispatcher — uncovered surface.

### G2 — Use the init primitives from `@shardworks/nexus-core` directly

```ts
import { createInitialGuildConfig, writeGuildConfig, VERSION } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';

const guildConfig = createInitialGuildConfig(name, pinnedFrameworkVersion, model);
writeGuildConfig(testGuildPath, guildConfig);
fs.writeFileSync(path.join(testGuildPath, 'package.json'), JSON.stringify({
  name: `guild-${name}`,
  private: true,
  version: '0.0.0',
  type: 'module',
  dependencies: {
    '@shardworks/nexus': pinnedFrameworkSpec,   // the trial-pinned spec
    ...trialPluginPins,                          // all plugins from manifest
  },
}, null, 2) + '\n');
fs.writeFileSync(path.join(testGuildPath, '.gitignore'), 'node_modules/\n.nexus/\n');
await execFile('npm', ['install'], { cwd: testGuildPath });
// From here: <testGuildPath>/node_modules/.bin/nsg ...
```

- **Version**: trial-pinned. `pinnedFrameworkSpec` comes from the manifest. The `VERSION` we pass to `createInitialGuildConfig` is also the manifest pin (extracted from the spec — for `1.2.3` it's `1.2.3`; for git URLs we may write the full spec).
- **Single npm install pass**: instead of `nsg init` + N plugin-install loops, we install everything in one resolve. Faster.
- **Dev-guard avoided**: we're not calling init; we write what we need directly.
- **Drift risk**: if `nsg init` learns to do additional setup later (write a database template, scaffold a directory), G2's reimplementation won't pick it up.

### H — npx for the one-time bootstrap

```ts
await execFile('npx', [
  '--yes',
  '-p', `@shardworks/nexus@${pinnedSpec}`,
  'nsg', 'init', testGuildPath, '--name', name,
]);
// Then plugin install loop using <testGuildPath>/node_modules/.bin/nsg
```

- **Version**: trial-pinned via npx's `-p`. Init runs at the manifest's pinned version, with that version's `VERSION` constant.
- **Mirrors user UX exactly**: the laboratory does what a real user would type. If init's behavior changes upstream, trials inherit the change automatically through the pinned version.
- **One-time tax**: cold-cache npm install (~2-5s); warm-cache reuse on subsequent trials with the same pin.
- **Dev-iteration constraint**: when pin is `git+file:///workspace/nexus#<sha>`, npx clones from that path/sha and runs the binstub. The dev binstub points at `./src/cli.ts` which won't run with plain node. **Trials in dev require the framework to be built first** (so `dist/cli.js` exists) — `cd /workspace/nexus && pnpm build && git commit && <use that sha>`.

## My read

I think **H is the right answer** and your "real users do it that way" framing is the deciding factor.

The architectural value: **the laboratory does what a real user would do.** That's not just stylistic — it's what makes trials reproducible by users who don't have a lab-host with the laboratory plugin installed. They'd `npx @shardworks/nexus@<pin> init`, install the plugins, post a commission. If the laboratory uses *exactly that mechanism* for its bootstrap, the recorded manifest is something a user can replay manually with `npx`.

Costs:
- **Cold-cache slowness**: real but one-time per (machine × pin). Lab-hosts run trials repeatedly; cache hit rate is high.
- **Network requirement**: real, but lab-hosts already need network for any nontrivial trial (npm install of trial plugins needs it anyway).
- **Dev-build step**: documented, aligns with "trials test built artifacts."

vs G2's "we write package.json ourselves":
- Faster, no network, no subprocess for init
- But the laboratory is now silently keeping pace with init's evolution
- And the apparatus is doing something users can't easily replicate

vs G1's "import the handler":
- Has all of G2's drift problem (we'd have to patch around the dev-guard)
- Plus the public-surface coupling

## My recommended flow

```
PHASE 1 — bootstrap (npx, one-time per trial):
  1. npx -p @shardworks/nexus@<pinnedSpec> nsg init <testGuild> --name <name>
     (creates guild.json, package.json with framework dep, .gitignore, runs npm install)

PHASE 2 — plugin install loop (test guild's nsg):
  2. <testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg --guild-root <X> plugin install <pin>
     (one shellout per trial plugin — same as today's loop, just different binary)

PHASE 3 — runtime (test guild's nsg):
  3. <testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg --guild-root <X> codex add ...
  4. <testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg --guild-root <X> commission-post ...
  5. <testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg --guild-root <X> writ-show ...
```

Lab-host's `nsg` is invoked **zero times** during a trial. The lab-host doesn't need a local CLI install at all — it only needs node + npm/npx (which the framework requires anyway for plugin installs).

Side benefits:
- The "where does nsg come from" question reduces to: "Phase 1 gets it via npx; phase 2-3 uses what Phase 1 installed."
- Cross-version trials work without special-casing — npx just resolves to whatever the manifest pinned.
- The `frameworkVersion` recorded in `lab.probe-trial-context` is now an authoritative input, not an observation.

## Two open questions before I implement

1. **`cliVersion` field on the manifest** — I propose calling it `frameworkVersion` since the package is `@shardworks/nexus` (the framework, not just the CLI). Reads cleanly:
   ```yaml
   frameworkVersion: '1.2.3'
   # or for dev:
   # frameworkVersion: 'git+file:///workspace/nexus#a1b2c3d4'
   ```
   Cascade: manifest field → laboratory's installed `@shardworks/nexus-core` version → fail loud if neither resolvable.

2. **Plugin install loop**: should this be the `nsg plugin install` shellout pattern (one call per plugin) or a single bulk `npm install` of all pins? Bulk is faster and avoids N node-startups; per-plugin matches what `nsg plugin install` actually does (probably runs `npm install` plus updates guild.json's `plugins` array). Looking at `init.ts`, plugin install probably has more side effects than just npm install, so per-plugin is safer. I'll keep the existing loop unless you say otherwise.

Want me to land Option F as a follow-up commit using H for bootstrap, or sleep on it?

content: let's just do per plugin. may add matching to nsg cmd later

2. **Resolution at trial-post time** (not engine time): manifest → lab-host's `VERSION` from `@shardworks/nexus-core` → fail loud if both unresolvable. Resolved value lands on the writ's `ext.laboratory.config` so the archive captures it.
3. **`_trial.frameworkVersion`** injected by the phase orchestrators alongside `slug`/`writId`/`fixtureId`.
4. **`lab.guild-setup`**: bootstrap via `npx -p @shardworks/nexus@<spec> nsg init`, then per-plugin loop via `<testGuildPath>/node_modules/.bin/nsg`.
5. **`lab.commission-post-xguild` + `wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild`**: use the test guild's local nsg.

1	/**
2	 * lab.guild-setup / lab.guild-teardown — fixture engines for a per-trial
3	 * test guild.
4	 *
5	 * MOTIVATION
6	 * ──────────
7	 * A trial wants a fresh, disposable guild instance to commission work
8	 * into. The guild needs:
9	 *   - The right plugins installed (varies per trial: every plugin the
10	 *     test workload exercises must be there).
11	 *   - The codexes from upstream fixtures registered (so the test guild
12	 *     knows about them).
13	 *   - Optional `guild.json` overlay (e.g. configuring patron role for
14	 *     astrolabe).
15	 *   - Optional file copies (e.g. a custom prompt template for animator).
16	 *
17	 * SETUP FLOW
18	 * ──────────
19	 * 1. Validate givens; resolve guildName + guildPath.
20	 * 2. Refuse if the target dir already exists (idempotency / safety).
21	 * 3. `nsg init <guildPath>`.
22	 * 4. For each plugin pin: `nsg --guild-root <guildPath> plugin install
23	 *    <name>@<version>`.
24	 * 5. Discover codexes from `context.upstream`: any upstream yields with
25	 *    `codexName` + `remoteUrl` (the codex-fixture's yields shape) get
26	 *    registered via `nsg --guild-root <guildPath> codex add`. Walks all
27	 *    upstream entries — no manifest-level dependsOn declaration of
28	 *    "this is the codex" needed.
29	 * 6. Deep-merge `givens.config` into `<guildPath>/guild.json`.
30	 * 7. Copy each `givens.files[]` entry from `sourcePath` (absolute only
31	 *    in v1) to `<guildPath>/<guildPath>` (per-entry guildPath).
32	 * 8. Yield guildPath, pluginsResolved, codexesAdded, filesCopied.
33	 *
34	 * Failure handling: any error mid-setup triggers best-effort rollback
35	 * (rm -rf the guild dir) and re-throws.
36	 *
37	 * TEARDOWN FLOW
38	 * ─────────────
39	 * 1. Validate givens; resolve guildPath.
40	 * 2. Archive-safety check (same as codex-teardown).
41	 * 3. `rm -rf <guildPath>`.
42	 * 4. Yield `{ removed: true, guildPath }`.
43	 *
44	 * GIVENS (setup AND teardown — fixture givens are shared)
45	 * ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
46	 *   guildName : string?  — Optional. The test guild's identity name.
47	 *                          Defaults to `<slug>-<writId-tail>` from the
48	 *                          framework-injected `_trial` context (matches
49	 *                          the codex-fixture's default-naming convention).
50	 *   guildPath : string?  — Optional. Absolute path for the test guild dir.
51	 *                          Defaults to
52	 *                          `<labHostGuild>/.nexus/laboratory/guilds/<guildName>/`.
53	 *   plugins   : Array<{name, version}>?  — Optional. Plugin pins to install.
54	 *                          Each entry is shelled out as
55	 *                          `nsg plugin install <name>@<version>`. Version
56	 *                          accepts npm semver, dist-tags, git URLs with
57	 *                          `#<sha>`, and local paths — passed through to
58	 *                          npm unchanged.
59	 *   config    : object?   — Optional. Deep-merged into the guild's
60	 *                          `guild.json` after init.
61	 *   files     : Array<{sourcePath, guildPath}>?  — Optional. File copies.
62	 *                          sourcePath must be absolute in v1 (throws on
63	 *                          relative). guildPath is relative to the guild
64	 *                          root.
65	 *
66	 * YIELDS (setup)
67	 * ──────────────
68	 *   {
69	 *     guildName: string,
70	 *     guildPath: string,
71	 *     pluginsResolved: Array<{ name: string; version: string }>,
72	 *     codexesAdded: Array<{ codexName: string; remoteUrl: string }>,
73	 *     filesCopied: Array<{ sourcePath: string; guildPath: string }>,
74	 *   }
75	 *
76	 * YIELDS (teardown)
77	 * ─────────────────
78	 *   { removed: true, guildName: string, guildPath: string }
79	 */

file.content:
/**
 * lab.guild-setup / lab.guild-teardown — fixture engines for a per-trial
 * test guild.
 *
 * MOTIVATION
 * ──────────
 * A trial wants a fresh, disposable guild instance to commission work
 * into. The guild needs:
 *   - The right plugins installed (varies per trial: every plugin the
 *     test workload exercises must be there).
 *   - The codexes from upstream fixtures registered (so the test guild
 *     knows about them).
 *   - Optional `guild.json` overlay (e.g. configuring patron role for
 *     astrolabe).
 *   - Optional file copies (e.g. a custom prompt template for animator).
 *
 * SETUP FLOW
 * ──────────
 * 1. Validate givens; resolve guildName + guildPath.
 * 2. Refuse if the target dir already exists (idempotency / safety).
 * 3. `nsg init <guildPath>`.
 * 4. For each plugin pin: `nsg --guild-root <guildPath> plugin install
 *    <name>@<version>`.
 * 5. Discover codexes from `context.upstream`: any upstream yields with
 *    `codexName` + `remoteUrl` (the codex-fixture's yields shape) get
 *    registered via `nsg --guild-root <guildPath> codex add`. Walks all
 *    upstream entries — no manifest-level dependsOn declaration of
 *    "this is the codex" needed.
 * 6. Deep-merge `givens.config` into `<guildPath>/guild.json`.
 * 7. Copy each `givens.files[]` entry from `sourcePath` (absolute only
 *    in v1) to `<guildPath>/<guildPath>` (per-entry guildPath).
 * 8. Yield guildPath, pluginsResolved, codexesAdded, filesCopied.
 *
 * Failure handling: any error mid-setup triggers best-effort rollback
 * (rm -rf the guild dir) and re-throws.
 *
 * TEARDOWN FLOW
 * ─────────────
 * 1. Validate givens; resolve guildPath.
 * 2. Archive-safety check (same as codex-teardown).
 * 3. `rm -rf <guildPath>`.
 * 4. Yield `{ removed: true, guildPath }`.
 *
 * GIVENS (setup AND teardown — fixture givens are shared)
 * ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
 *   guildName : string?  — Optional. The test guild's identity name.
 *                          Defaults to `<slug>-<writId-tail>` from the
 *                          framework-injected `_trial` context (matches
 *                          the codex-fixture's default-naming convention).
 *   guildPath : string?  — Optional. Absolute path for the test guild dir.
 *                          Defaults to
 *                          `<labHostGuild>/.nexus/laboratory/guilds/<guildName>/`.
 *   plugins   : Array<{name, version}>?  — Optional. Plugin pins to install.
 *                          Each entry is shelled out as
 *                          `nsg plugin install <name>@<version>`. Version
 *                          accepts npm semver, dist-tags, git URLs with
 *                          `#<sha>`, and local paths — passed through to
 *                          npm unchanged.
 *   config    : object?   — Optional. Deep-merged into the guild's
 *                          `guild.json` after init.
 *   files     : Array<{sourcePath, guildPath}>?  — Optional. File copies.
 *                          sourcePath must be absolute in v1 (throws on
 *                          relative). guildPath is relative to the guild
 *                          root.
 *
 * YIELDS (setup)
 * ──────────────
 *   {
 *     guildName: string,
 *     guildPath: string,
 *     pluginsResolved: Array<{ name: string; version: string }>,
 *     codexesAdded: Array<{ codexName: string; remoteUrl: string }>,
 *     filesCopied: Array<{ sourcePath: string; guildPath: string }>,
 *   }
 *
 * YIELDS (teardown)
 * ─────────────────
 *   { removed: true, guildName: string, guildPath: string }
 */

import { execFile as execFileCb } from 'node:child_process';
import { promisify } from 'node:util';
import { existsSync } from 'node:fs';
import { copyFile, mkdir, readFile, rm, writeFile } from 'node:fs/promises';
import path from 'node:path';

input.new_string:
* SETUP FLOW
 * ──────────
 * 1. Validate givens; resolve guildName, guildPath, frameworkVersion.
 * 2. Refuse if the target dir already exists (idempotency / safety).
 * 3. **Bootstrap (one-time, npx):**
 *    `npx -p @shardworks/nexus@<frameworkVersion> nsg init <guildPath>
 *    --name <guildName>`. This mirrors how a real user would create a
 *    guild from scratch and binds the test guild to the trial-pinned
 *    framework version (the version-true `init` runs against the
 *    version-true `VERSION` constant). After bootstrap,
 *    `<guildPath>/node_modules/.bin/nsg` exists.
 * 4. For each plugin pin: `<guildPath>/node_modules/.bin/nsg
 *    --guild-root <guildPath> plugin install <name>@<version>`.
 * 5. Discover codexes from `context.upstream`: any upstream yields with
 *    `codexName` + `remoteUrl` (the codex-fixture's yields shape) get
 *    registered via the test guild's local nsg. Walks all upstream
 *    entries — no manifest-level dependsOn declaration of "this is
 *    the codex" needed.
 * 6. Deep-merge `givens.config` into `<guildPath>/guild.json`.
 * 7. Copy each `givens.files[]` entry from `sourcePath` (absolute only
 *    in v1) to `<guildPath>/<guildPath>` (per-entry guildPath).
 * 8. Yield guildPath, pluginsResolved, codexesAdded, filesCopied.
 *
 * After bootstrap (step 3), the lab-host's `nsg` is **not** invoked
 * again for this trial. Steps 4-5 use the test guild's locally-
 * installed CLI; the scenario engine (commission-post-xguild) and
 * its sibling do the same. The lab-host doesn't need a global or
 * local `nsg` install for the laboratory to work — only `npx` (which
 * ships with Node).

 * Tests for the trial manifest parser and validator.
 *
 * Covers:
 *   - YAML syntactic errors (helpful message; no zod tracebacks)
 *   - Empty / non-map manifests rejected
 *   - Schema rejections (missing required fields, slug shape,
 *     engineId required, dependsOn type)
 *   - Defaults (empty fixtures, empty probes, empty givens)
 *   - Cross-field: fixture DAG (cycles, unknown deps, dups)
 *   - Cross-field: probe id uniqueness
 *   - Round-trip: manifest → writ-post + trial config
 *   - Title default ("Trial: <slug>")
 *   - description → body, missing description → empty body
 *   - parentId / codex pass-through
 */

`;

slug: orientation-suppression-strong
title: P3 — orientation suppression, strong-prompt variant
description: |
  Tests whether an imperative anti-orientation directive in the
  implementer handoff produces productive work in fewer than five
  turns. Captures the test-guild's animator/sessions book for
  cost/turn-count analysis.

fixtures:
  - id: codex
    engineId: lab.codex-setup
    givens:
      # Any git clone source: owner/name, full URL, or absolute path.
      # Local-bare codex flow — no GitHub round-trip.
      upstreamRepo: /workspace/nexus-mk2
      baseSha: 4b50b6542aa07bd7b74dca1a1d581f90788d4d47
      # codexName auto-defaults to <slug>-<writId-tail> from
      # framework-injected _trial. Override only when needed.

  - id: test-guild
    engineId: lab.guild-setup
    dependsOn: [codex]
    givens:
      # Plugin pins MUST be stable identifiers (exact semver, git+url
      # with SHA fragment, github-shorthand#sha, or registry tarball).
      # The manifest CLI rejects file:/link:/range/dist-tag forms at
      # load time. See "Plugin pin reproducibility" below.
      plugins:
        - name: '@shardworks/tools-apparatus'
          version: '1.2.3'
        - name: '@shardworks/codexes-apparatus'
          version: '1.2.3'
        - name: '@shardworks/stacks-apparatus'
          version: '1.2.3'
        - name: '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus'

file.content:
The SHA pins the artifact deterministically; the URL just tells the
resolver where to fetch from. No need to push to the remote.

The validator runs in `lab-trial-post`'s manifest-load step. Failed
pins surface with the issue path (e.g.
`fixtures.0.givens.plugins.2.version`) and a specific reason for
each rejection — multiple bad pins in one manifest are reported
together.

### After posting

```sh
# Post the trial; the rig fires immediately (writ goes new → open).
nsg lab trial-post manifest.yaml

# Watch the rig flow.
nsg writ list --type trial
nsg writ-show <trialId>           # writ-level view
nsg rig list --writ <trialId>     # the rig executing it

# After the trial completes:
nsg lab trial-show <trialId>      # archive metadata + probe summaries
nsg lab trial-extract <trialId> --to /tmp/extract  # materialize captured data
nsg lab trial-export-book <trialId> --book animator/sessions  # streaming JSONL
```

### Authoring tips

- **Codex base SHA picks the snapshot.** The codex-setup engine
  clones the upstream repo, checks out `baseSha`, and pushes that
  to the trial's local-bare repo. Pick the SHA you want the test
  guild to start from — typically the head of whatever branch
  embodies the variant under test.
- **Plugin pins must be stable.** See "Plugin pin reproducibility"

input.new_string:
The validator runs in `lab-trial-post`'s manifest-load step. Failed
pins surface with the issue path (e.g.
`fixtures.0.givens.plugins.2.version`) and a specific reason for
each rejection — multiple bad pins in one manifest are reported
together.

### Test-guild bootstrap (no global `nsg` required)

`lab.guild-setup` does not depend on a global or lab-host-local
`nsg` install. The bootstrap sequence is:

1. **`npx -p @shardworks/nexus@<frameworkVersion> nsg init <testGuild>`**
   — runs the trial-pinned framework's `init` against the trial-pinned
   `VERSION` constant. This mirrors how a real user creates a guild
   from scratch and binds the test guild to the trial-pinned version.
   On a cold cache, this is a one-time `npm install` of the framework
   into npx's per-package cache; subsequent trials with the same pin
   hit the cache.

2. After init, the test guild has `@shardworks/nexus@<frameworkVersion>`
   installed in `<testGuild>/node_modules`, and the binstub at
   `<testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg` is the version-true CLI.

3. **All subsequent shellouts** (plugin install loop, codex add,
   commission-post, writ-show) use that local nsg, not anything on
   the lab-host's PATH or in the lab-host's `node_modules`.

The lab-host needs only Node + npx — both ship with any standard Node
install. No global `nsg`, no lab-host-side `@shardworks/nexus` package,
no npm/pnpm version coordination.

**Resolving `frameworkVersion`:**

- **Manifest field set** → use it.
- **Manifest field absent + lab-host has a real VERSION** → fall back
  to the lab-host's installed `@shardworks/nexus-core` VERSION. This
  case happens transparently in production where the lab-host is
  npm-installed.
- **Manifest field absent + lab-host VERSION is `0.0.0` (dev source)**
  → fail loud. The trial-post tool tells the author to set
  `frameworkVersion` explicitly.

The resolved value is written back into the trial writ's
`ext.laboratory.config` before the writ transitions to `open`, so the
archive snapshot captures the actual pin used.

**Dev iteration on framework source.** When pinning to a local commit
(`git+file:///workspace/nexus#<sha>`), the framework source must be
**built** (`dist/cli.js` exists). The `nsg` binstub in a fresh test
guild's `node_modules` points at the package's `bin` field — the
non-published variant is `./src/cli.ts` (TypeScript), which won't run
with plain node. Build before posting:

```sh
cd /workspace/nexus
pnpm build
git commit -am 'wip: testing change X'
# pin the trial to that sha
nsg lab trial-post my-trial.yaml
```

This trades the dev-time live-source loop for trial reproducibility.
Trials are archived as inputs you can re-run later — that's only
possible if the artifacts they reference are content-addressed.

### After posting

toolUseResult.oldString: ### 2. Execution — five-phase rig template
toolUseResult.newString:
### Test-guild bootstrap

Trials are reproducibility artifacts: the manifest is archived and
must re-resolve to the same artifacts months later. The Laboratory
enforces this in two ways:

**Stable plugin pins.** The manifest CLI rejects unstable pin forms
at load time — `file:`/`link:`/`workspace:`/version ranges/dist-tags
all fail with specific reasons. Whitelist: exact semver,
`git+<url>#<sha>` (any scheme including `git+file://`), GitHub
shorthand `foo/bar#<sha>`, registry tarball URLs.

**Version-true bootstrap.** `lab.guild-setup` runs:

```sh
npx -p @shardworks/nexus@<frameworkVersion> nsg init <testGuild>
```

— the trial-pinned framework's `init` against the trial-pinned
`VERSION` constant. After this, the test guild has the framework in
its own `node_modules`; the binstub at `<testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg`
is the version-true CLI, used for every subsequent shellout (plugin
install, codex add, commission-post, writ-show). The lab-host needs
only Node + npx — no global `nsg`, no lab-host-side framework
install, no version coordination between lab-host and test guild.

`frameworkVersion` resolution: manifest field if set, otherwise
fall back to the lab-host's installed `@shardworks/nexus-core`
VERSION. Refuses to fall back to `'0.0.0'` (dev source) — manifest
authors must pin explicitly when running on a dev lab-host. The
resolved value is written back into the trial writ's
`ext.laboratory.config` before publish so the archive snapshot
captures the actual pin used.

### 2. Execution — five-phase rig template

content.text:
Done. Summary of what landed:

## Option F + H bootstrap

**Manifest:**
- New optional top-level `frameworkVersion` field
- Validated by `isStablePin` (same stable-pin rules as plugin pins)
- 8 new tests for the field + the `manifestToWritShape` pass-through

**`tools/trial-post.ts`:**
- Resolution cascade: manifest field → lab-host's `@shardworks/nexus-core` VERSION → fail loud
- Refuses to fall back to `'0.0.0'` (dev source) — manifest must pin explicitly when running on dev lab-host
- Writes the resolved value back into the trial config before stamping the writ; archive snapshot captures the pin actually used

**`engines/guild-fixture.ts` (`lab.guild-setup`):**
- Bootstrap: `npx -p @shardworks/nexus@<frameworkVersion> nsg init <testGuild> --name <name>`
- After bootstrap, all shellouts use `<testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg`
- Per-plugin install loop preserved (your call — matches what `nsg plugin install` does, side effects beyond just `npm install`)
- Helpful error when bootstrap doesn't produce the expected binstub (e.g. dev-guard fired)

**`engines/scenario-xguild.ts` (`lab.commission-post-xguild`, `lab.wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild`):**
- New exported `resolveLocalNsg(testGuildPath, designId)` helper
- `commission-post` and `writ-show` shellouts use the test guild's local nsg

**`engines/phases.ts`:**
- `InjectedTrialContext.frameworkVersion?: string`
- All four `build*Graft` functions populate it
- `withTrialContext` strips undefined fields so tests can deep-equal against the minimal shape

**Documentation:**
- README's "Authoring trials" gains a new "Test-guild bootstrap" subsection covering the npx mechanic, the frameworkVersion resolution cascade, and the dev-build-first caveat
- Minimal manifest example now shows `frameworkVersion: '1.2.3'`
- Architecture doc gets a "Test-guild bootstrap" section in the same shape

**Test count:** 167 → 201 (stable-pin work) → 209 (frameworkVersion work). All passing, typecheck clean.

## Two things you should know about

1. **Live verification is now blocked on a publishable framework.** When you next try to run a trial against vibers, it'll attempt `npx -p @shardworks/nexus@<spec>`. For that to work, `@shardworks/nexus` must be either (a) published to npm at the pinned version, or (b) pinned via `git+file:///workspace/nexus#<sha>` AND the source built (`dist/cli.js` exists). The wrapper-on-PATH approach won't help here. This is expected — it's the cost of reproducibility — but it changes what "live verify" looks like next time.

2. **The dev-build constraint may bite first time.** Pinning `git+file:///workspace/nexus#<sha>` works, but only after `cd /workspace/nexus && pnpm build && git commit`. The first manual trial you run will likely hit this — guild-setup will fail with "expected `<testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg` after bootstrap but it is missing" and the message points at the dev-guard. The error is intentionally specific so you don't waste time debugging.

Want me to keep going on anything else, or is this a good stopping point?

content.text:
Quick accounting against the original handoff plus what's been added mid-stream:

## Done

From the original handoff:
1. ✅ **Probe registry + extract dispatch** (`c-momkil4p`) — type-guard pattern over Fabricator's existing registry
2. ✅ **Three standard probes** (`c-momaa3w7`) — trial-context, stacks-dump, git-range
3. ✅ **Archive engine** (`c-momkqtn5`) — plus tightened teardown safety check
4. ✅ **CLI tools** — trial-show, trial-extract, trial-export-book (joining trial-post; `nsg lab` auto-grouping activates)
5. ✅ **Codified smoke test** (`c-momaa75l`) — synthetic-engine pipeline test
6. ✅ **Retire infra spec** (`c-momaa8mk`) — archived to `docs/archive/deprecated-docs/`
7. ✅ **Documentation** (`c-momaaa3t`) — README authoring guide + architecture doc

Added mid-conversation:
8. ✅ **Stable-pin enforcement** — manifest validator rejects unstable pin forms at load time
9. ✅ **Option F + H** — per-trial `frameworkVersion` pin, npx bootstrap, test guild's local nsg for everything else

## Not done (and why)

**Port the first real-world trial (`c-momaab8y`)** — the only original-handoff item that's still live. Deferred from the start because it requires:
- Vibers' daemon restart to pick up the new engine + book registrations
- A built `@shardworks/nexus` (so `dist/cli.js` exists when npx-installs from `git+file:///workspace/nexus#<sha>`) — or alternatively a published version to pin to
- Picking the actual P3 candidate (X016 orientation suppression was suggested) and authoring its manifest
- Live observation + iteration on whatever the first run surfaces

This is the capstone — runs the apparatus on real research work and produces the first findings via the new pipeline. Expect a small iteration loop on first run since synthetic tests can't catch every assumption.

## Open follow-up clicks (filed but not addressed)

- **`c-momm4abc` — Failed-trial orphan cleanup.** When `guild-setup` fails after `codex-setup` succeeds, teardowns don't run. Codex stays registered, bare repo stays on disk. Two design directions: setup engines grab a release-on-rig-terminal handle, or teardowns run unconditionally on rig failure. Surfaced during the earlier live verifies; not blocking but accumulates cruft.
- **`c-momm4i15` — Daemon ↔ CLI Scriptorium state drift.** `nsg codex remove` from the CLI updates `guild.json` but the running daemon's in-memory Scriptorium isn't notified; the daemon's next write reverts the orphans. Cosmetic. Vibers currently has three of these.

## Minor refinement worth flagging

`lab.probe-trial-context` captures `frameworkVersion` from the **lab-host's** `guildConfig.nexus`. With the new bootstrap, the **test guild's** framework version (= the trial-pinned version) is the more interesting reproducibility-relevant fact, and it's already captured via `manifestSnapshot.frameworkVersion`. The probe's lab-host-VERSION field is now arguably misleading — readers might think it's "the version the trial ran against" when it's "the version of the apparatus doing the orchestration."

Two clean fixes:
- Rename the probe's field to `labHostFrameworkVersion` to disambiguate from `manifestSnapshot.frameworkVersion`.
- Or capture both: `labHostFrameworkVersion` (lab-host's) + `testGuildFrameworkVersion` (the resolved manifest pin, redundant with manifestSnapshot but explicit).

Not urgent — both values are recoverable from the existing fields. Worth a small follow-up commit before the first real trial runs, so analysis queries don't get the wrong answer to "what version did this run against."

## Live verification blocked on you

Things only you can do (involve daemon restart or live-trial posting):

1. **Restart vibers' daemon** to pick up the new engine/book registrations.
2. **Live-verify the four-tool `nsg lab` auto-grouping.** All four commands should surface as `nsg lab <subcommand>`.
3. **First end-to-end trial against vibers** with the new bootstrap. Will exercise:
   - npx-based init with a real `frameworkVersion` pin
   - Plugin install loop via the test guild's local nsg
   - The full probe → archive → extract pipeline against real data
4. **Decide on the framework-version-capture refinement** above before the first trial generates archive rows we'd then have to interpret-around.

Net: implementation is feature-complete against the handoff. The only outstanding work is the first real-world trial port and live verification — both gated on you being at the keyboard.

Given the daemon constraint (the test guild has no running daemon, so a full implementer rig can't progress), I'll scope the first trial as **apparatus validation** — the workload is a real commission post with `waitForTerminal: false`. That validates the full pipeline (npx bootstrap, plugin install loop, commission post, probes, archive, extract) producing real data, while deferring "actually run the implementer and measure orientation tax" to a follow-up that adds daemon lifecycle to `lab.guild-setup`.

Let me set up the experiment:

input.content:
# X016 — Orientation Suppression

**Status:** Draft, Trial 1 (apparatus validation) authored 2026-05-01.

**Parent click:** `c-mok4qh81` — Idea #17 from the cost-optimization
landscape.

## Research question

When a fresh implementer session enters mid-flow (i.e., after a
handoff from a prior session), how many turns elapse before it
produces productive work? Does an imperative anti-orientation
directive in the brief reduce that turn count to <5?

## Background

The X010 H1 piece-session experiment (per-task implementer
checkpointing) failed because each fresh session paid an
"orientation tax": re-read the spec, re-grep the codebase, re-state
the plan, re-validate prior commits before producing any new output.
Total turn count expanded 3.5×, and the savings from cache-read
accumulation were swamped.

For idea #15 (checkpoint-and-fresh-session implement architecture)
to win, total turn count post-handoff must stay under ~1.3×
monolithic, which means each post-handoff session must produce
productive work in <5 turns. This experiment measures whether an
imperative directive in the implementer's prompt suppresses the
orientation tax.

## Hypothesis

**H1.** Adding an imperative directive ("Begin work immediately. Do
not summarize the task before starting. Make your first turn a code
change.") to the implementer's brief reduces median turns-to-first-
productive-edit from N (baseline) to fewer than 5.

## Variants

| variant | description |
|---|---|
| baseline | implementer brief carries no orientation-suppression directive |
| strong-prompt | brief carries the imperative directive above |

## Apparatus

This is the first real-world trial running through the **Laboratory
apparatus** (`packages/laboratory/`). Trial manifests live under
`manifests/`; archived data lives in vibers' `lab-trial-*` books;
extracted directories under `artifacts/<trial-slug>/`.

## Design notes

### Phase 1 — apparatus validation (this trial)

The first run validates the apparatus pipeline end-to-end without
attempting to execute a full implementer session. The test guild
posts a commission via `lab.commission-post-xguild` with
`waitForTerminal: false` — the writ lands but no rig is driven to
completion.

**Why phase 1 doesn't measure orientation behavior**: a full
implementer rig requires the test guild's Spider crawl loop to be
running, which currently means a clockworks daemon. The Laboratory
does not yet manage daemon lifecycle for test guilds; adding that is
a follow-up. Phase 1 instead validates:

- Codex setup (npx-bootstrapped local-bare flow)
- Test-guild bootstrap via `npx -p @shardworks/nexus@<v> nsg init`
- Plugin install loop via `<testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg`
- Commission post lands (writ in `clerk/writs`)
- Probes capture: `lab.probe-stacks-dump` against the test guild's
  books, `lab.probe-trial-context` for rig metadata,
  `lab.probe-git-range` for codex commits (will be empty since no
  rig sealed)
- Archive engine writes `lab-trial-archives` row
- Teardown safety check passes (archive row exists)
- `nsg lab trial-extract` materializes captured data

### Phase 2 — full execution (future)

Once daemon lifecycle is added to `lab.guild-setup`, the manifest
can switch to `waitForTerminal: true` and the implementer rig will
actually run. At that point:

- baseline + strong-prompt variants both run as separate trials
- `lab.probe-stacks-dump` captures `animator/sessions` rows
  (turn counts, cost, durations) for each variant
- `lab.probe-git-range` captures the implementer's commits to
  measure productive-output timing
- Comparative analysis happens sanctum-side via
  `nsg lab trial-export-book <trialId> --book animator/sessions`
  piped into a DuckDB query

## Trial-shape constraints

- **Codex base SHA**: pinned to a specific sanctum SHA at trial
  authoring time (locks the test guild against a stable code state).
- **Framework version**: pinned to a published `@shardworks/nexus`
  version (currently `0.1.292`).
- **Plugin set**: vibers' standard set minus optional plugins
  (lattice/lattice-discord/oculus/parlour/sentinel are not
  installed in the test guild).

## References

- `docs/archive/deprecated-docs/experimental-infrastructure-setup-and-artifacts.md`
  — predecessor design that the apparatus replaces.
- `packages/laboratory/README.md` — apparatus authoring guide.
- Click `c-mok4nke6` — cost-optimization landscape (parent of #17).
- Click `c-mok4qh81` — idea #17, this experiment's parent.
- Click `c-modxxtu6` — idea #15, the architecture this binds to.

## Status log

- 2026-05-01: Spec drafted. Phase 1 manifest authored
  (`manifests/baseline-apparatus-validation.yaml`). First trial
  posted as the Laboratory apparatus's first real-world use.

content.content: File created successfully at: /workspace/nexus-mk2/experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/spec.md

input.content:
# Add a multiplySafely utility

Add a TypeScript function `multiplySafely(a: number, b: number):
number | null` that returns `null` when the multiplication would
overflow `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`, and otherwise returns `a * b`.

## Acceptance criteria

- Function lives in `src/util/numeric.ts` (create the file if it
  doesn't exist).
- Function exported from `src/util/numeric.ts`.
- One unit test covering: a typical successful multiplication, an
  overflow case, and the boundary case where the result is exactly
  `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`.
- Tests pass under `pnpm test`.
- Build passes under `pnpm build`.

## Context

This is a small bounded task chosen for the Laboratory apparatus's
first real-world trial run. The brief is small enough to complete
quickly in an implementer session and large enough to produce a
real animator session record + at least one codex commit.

For phase 1 of the X016 experiment (apparatus validation), this
brief is posted but the test guild does not have a running daemon
to execute the rig. The brief lands as a writ but no implementer
session runs. Phase 2 will execute it.

slug: x016-baseline-validation
title: X016 Trial 1 — apparatus validation (baseline brief, no rig execution)
description: |
  First real-world Laboratory trial. Validates the apparatus pipeline
  end-to-end against vibers as the lab-host, posting a real
  commission into a fresh test guild via the npx-bootstrapped flow.

  waitForTerminal=false because the test guild has no running daemon
  to drive the rig; the trial validates apparatus mechanics, not
  implementer behavior. Full execution (phase 2) deferred pending
  daemon-lifecycle support in lab.guild-setup.

  See experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/spec.md for the full
  research design.

frameworkVersion: '0.1.292'

fixtures:
  - id: codex
    engineId: lab.codex-setup
    givens:
      # Local-bare codex flow — vibers clones from this path, checks
      # out the pinned SHA, and pushes to a per-trial bare repo.
      upstreamRepo: /workspace/nexus-mk2
      baseSha: 2e8a59e5f62a02594410e6a101b0141d849157a8

  - id: test-guild
    engineId: lab.guild-setup
    dependsOn: [codex]
    givens:
      # Minimum plugin set for a test guild that can post a writ via
      # `nsg --guild-root <X> commission-post`. Stacks + clerk are the
      # backbone (writ persistence); codexes registers the trial codex;
      # tools dispatches the commission-post tool.
      plugins:
        - { name: '@shardworks/stacks-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/tools-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/codexes-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }

scenario:
  engineId: lab.commission-post-xguild
  givens:
    briefPath: /workspace/nexus-mk2/experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/briefs/baseline-task.md
    # No rig execution in phase 1 — the writ lands and we capture the
    # apparatus output without waiting on a session.
    waitForTerminal: false

probes:
  - id: context
    engineId: lab.probe-trial-context
    givens: {}
  - id: stacks
    engineId: lab.probe-stacks-dump
    givens: {}
  - id: commits
    engineId: lab.probe-git-range
    givens: {}

archive:
  engineId: lab.archive
  givens: {}

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Experiments Index

Maintained by Coco. Update when experiments are created, activated, or closed.

## Active

| # | Name | Research Question |
|---|---|---|
| [X006](X006-guild-metaphor/spec.md) | The Guild Metaphor | Does the guild metaphor improve agent performance and patron experience compared to plain instructions? |
| [X008](X008-patrons-hands/spec.md) | The Patron's Hands | How does the bootstrap period (Coco doing implementation) shape patron expectations when autonomous agents come online? |
| [X010](X010-staged-sessions/spec.md) | Staged Sessions | What is the relationship between session length, cost, and output quality for autonomous commissions? Phase 1 (cost curve) complete at original scale; Apr 29 addendum sharpens H4 into H4a/H4b/H4c sub-hypotheses (cost model + handoff thresholds) and adds H5 (inventory pure-read bloat). |
| [X011](X011-context-debt/spec.md) | Context Debt | How much context window is consumed by tool output never referenced again, and can we reduce it? Activated 2026-04-29 with the read-utilization instrument as its first artifact. |

## Ready (approved, not yet started)

| # | Name | Research Question |
|---|---|---|
| [X003](X003-commission-prompt-tuning/spec.md) | Commission Prompt Tuning | How do pre-commission instructions affect agent performance on identical tasks? |
| [X004](X004-iteration-context/spec.md) | Iteration Context | How does context about prior sessions affect quality and safety when an agent iterates on its own work? |
| [X009](X009-metaphor-as-instruction/spec.md) | Metaphor as Implicit Instruction | Does the guild metaphor carry actual behavioral weight, or is it decorative? |

## Draft (not yet approved)

| # | Name | Research Question |
|---|---|---|
| [X005](X005-greenfield-assembly/spec.md) | Greenfield Assembly | What if agents never iterate on existing code — only build net-new? |
| [X012](X012-agent-oriented-code/spec.md) | Agent-Oriented Code | Do conventional clean code principles still apply when agents are the primary authors? |
| [X014](X014-technical-spec-quality/spec.md) | Technical Spec Quality | Do technically detailed specs outperform mountain-quality specs — and does it depend on complexity? |
| [X015](X015-spec-detail-model-substitute/spec.md) | Spec Detail as Model Substitute | Can planning-pipeline specs reduce the model capability threshold, enabling Sonnet-class models to match Opus outcomes? |

## Complete

| # | Name | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| [X001](X001-fruit-delivery/spec.md) | Fruit Delivery | Complete |
| [X007](X007-first-contact/spec.md) | First Contact | Complete — H1 (manifest gap) confirmed |

## Superseded

| # | Name | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| [X002](X002-agent-session-launcher/spec.md) | Agent Session Launcher | Deliverable built as part of session refactor; experiment no longer needed |
| [X013](X013-commission-outcomes/spec.md) | Commission Outcomes | Spec generation became automated (Astrolabe pipeline), eliminating spec-quality variance; structured patron review (complexity, outcome, failure_mode, reviewed_at) was retired 2026-04-30. The four hypotheses depended on patron-set instrumentation that no longer exists. Historical dataset (150 patron-touched entries) preserved as a frozen baseline in the X013 and X008 artifacts dirs. |

# The lab-host (vibers) has multiple codexes registered; pick `nexus`
# explicitly. This is the codex the TRIAL WRIT is associated with on

description: |
toolUseResult.originalFile:
slug: x016-baseline-validation
title: X016 Trial 1 — apparatus validation (baseline brief, no rig execution)
description: |
  First real-world Laboratory trial. Validates the apparatus pipeline
  end-to-end against vibers as the lab-host, posting a real
  commission into a fresh test guild via the npx-bootstrapped flow.

  waitForTerminal=false because the test guild has no running daemon
  to drive the rig; the trial validates apparatus mechanics, not
  implementer behavior. Full execution (phase 2) deferred pending
  daemon-lifecycle support in lab.guild-setup.

  See experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/spec.md for the full
  research design.

frameworkVersion: '0.1.292'

fixtures:
  - id: codex
    engineId: lab.codex-setup
    givens:
      # Local-bare codex flow — vibers clones from this path, checks
      # out the pinned SHA, and pushes to a per-trial bare repo.
      upstreamRepo: /workspace/nexus-mk2
      baseSha: 2e8a59e5f62a02594410e6a101b0141d849157a8

  - id: test-guild
    engineId: lab.guild-setup
    dependsOn: [codex]
    givens:
      # Minimum plugin set for a test guild that can post a writ via
      # `nsg --guild-root <X> commission-post`. Stacks + clerk are the
      # backbone (writ persistence); codexes registers the trial codex;
      # tools dispatches the commission-post tool.
      plugins:
        - { name: '@shardworks/stacks-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/tools-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/codexes-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }

scenario:
  engineId: lab.commission-post-xguild
  givens:
    briefPath: /workspace/nexus-mk2/experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/briefs/baseline-task.md
    # No rig execution in phase 1 — the writ lands and we capture the
    # apparatus output without waiting on a session.
    waitForTerminal: false

probes:
  - id: context
    engineId: lab.probe-trial-context
    givens: {}
  - id: stacks
    engineId: lab.probe-stacks-dump
    givens: {}
  - id: commits
    engineId: lab.probe-git-range
    givens: {}

archive:
  engineId: lab.archive
  givens: {}

content.text:
Excellent — the apparatus pipeline got further than ever before! Codex setup ✅, **guild setup ✅** (validates the npx bootstrap + per-plugin install loop + codex registration into the test guild end-to-end), commission-post ✅, trial-context probe ✅. Then `probe-stacks-dump` failed with **"require is not defined"** — I used CommonJS `require('node:fs')` in an ESM module. Real bug; unit tests didn't catch it because that helper isn't called from the test path.

Let me fix it, clean up the orphan fixtures from this run, restart vibers' daemon to pick up the fix, and re-post:

- session: 927ab84f-c92d-4a65-94ac-a19daf97ee42
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "First real-world Laboratory trial caught a real bug in lab.probe-stacks-dump: `require is not defined` in an ESM module. The probe used `const fs = require('node:fs')` to read the test guild's guild.json synchronously; ESM modules don't have a CommonJS require available without createRequire. Replaced with proper `import { existsSync, readFileSync } from 'node:fs'` at the top of the file. Unit tests didn't catch it because the helper isn't called in any test path. The trial-1 run validated everything else: codex setup (npx-bootstrapped), guild setup (npx-bootstrapped + plugin install loop via test guild's local nsg + codex registration in test guild), commission-post-xguild (writ landed in test guild's clerk), probe-trial-context. Failure caught at probe-stacks-dump and cancelled probe-git-range, archive, and both teardowns (the orphan-cleanup grit click c-momm4abc — failure → no teardowns → orphan codex+guild on disk — fired exactly as predicted). Cleaned up the orphan codex and dirs manually after stopping vibers' daemon. Trial: w-mond5em5-2b2e5f3fc359, rig rig-mond5emz-ff53d439, completed-then-failed in 18s wallclock."
